applicants  from  state-run  medical  schools,  does  it permit  a state

v.  Painter,  339  U.S.  629  (1950).

to  deny  admission  altogether  to  white  students  on  racial
grounds,  while  letting  in far less-qualified  minority  applicants?
Does  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  mean  that  some  persons,  who
belong  to  groups  which  have  historically  been  discriminated
against,  are  now  to  be  more  equal  than  members  of  other
groups?  We  think  not.

The  minorities  who  benefit  from  preferential  treatment  are
commonly  termed  “disadvantaged.”  While  not  all  such  benefi-
ciaries  come  from  deprived  backgrounds,  all  of  them  may
indeed  be  said  to  be  disadvantaged  in  that  they  belong  to
groups  whose  members  are  stili  mistreated  by some  persons  out
of prejudice  and  bigotry,  even  though  they  also  often  receive
benefits  and  preferences  in employment  and  university  admis-
sions  and  financial  aid.
But  none  of  them  suffers  from  the
particular  disadvantage  with  which  they were  formerly  saddled
when  discriminatory  acts  against  them  were  not  considered
illegal.  Now,  Petitioners  would  argue,  only non-minorities  have
to  endure  that.

Before  the  adoption  of the  post-Civil  War  Constitutional
amendments,  America’s  blacks  enjoyed  little  in the  way  of civil
rights.  After  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  was  interpreted,  in
Plessy  v.  Ferguson,  163  U.S.  537  (1896),  as  allowing a state  to
provide  “separate  but equal”  treatment  for  blacks,  members  of
that  group  were  still  under  a  considerable  disadvantage,
although  they  did  not  have  the  problems  that  a  Bakke  faces
today.  For  him,  no  “separate  but  equal”  medical  school.  For
Bakke,  says  the  University  of California,  it need  not  provide  a
medical  education  at  all, even  though  he is far  better  qualified
than  most  if not  all  of  the  preferentially  admitted  minority
applicants.

Thus,  Bakke’s  plight  is  far  more  similar  to  that  of  the
blacks  before  the  advent  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment  than  it
is to  the  still-dreadful  situation  blacks  faced  between  1896  and
the  historic  abolition  of the  Plessy  rule  in  Brown  v.  Board  of

Clearly,  the
Education  of  Topeka,  347  US.  483  (1954).
University  is really  trying  to  make  the  Bakkes  of this  country
into  “constitutional  non-persons.”

In  order  for  Petitioners  to  prevail  in  this  case,  it  is
obviously  necessary  for  them  to  trumpet  forth  the  notion  that
various  kinds  of flagrant  discrimination  which  obviously  cannot
be  visited  upon  blacks  or  Hispanics  can,  with  complete  impu-
nity,  be inflicted  upon  members  of other  and  non-racial  minor-
ity groups,  and  upon  whites  in general.  That  proposition  bodes
ill  indeed  for  America’s  future.?

For  such  racial  minorities  as  blacks  and  Chicanos,  cer-
tainly,  the  right  to  be  free  from  discrimination  in  a  state-
administered  selective  process  is  now  a  fixed,  settled,  immu-
table  fact  of legal  life  in  this  country.  Abolishing  that  right  is
not  a  proper  subject  for  discussion;  to  seriously  discuss  such  a

All of us,  minority  or  majority,  must  be aware  that  once  the
quota  principle  is  accepted,  there  is  no  stopping  point,  quan-
titative  or  qualitative.
Consider,  for  example,  that  while  the
Davis  Medical  School  minority  quota  may  be only  16%,  that  of
the  same  University’s  School  of Law  at  Berkeley  (Boalt  Hall )
was  25%  until  1975,  and  is  now  224%.  Law  School  Deans’
Brief  on  Certiorari,  25.  And  as  far  back  as  six  years  ago,  the
faculty  of the  School  of Social  Work  at  Fresno  State  College  in
California  (now  California  State  University,  Fresno),  on
November  22,  1971,  approved  a resolution  that  it “should  have
as  a  goal  for  1972-73  an  incoming  graduate  class  consisting  of
one-third  black,  one-third  Chicano,  one-tenth  other  minorities,
and  the  remainder,  Caucasian  students.”  Under  these  minority
quotas,  which  totaled  about  77%,  only  about  23%  of  the
incoming  students  were  to  be white.  B.R.  Epstein  & A.  Forster,
Preferential  Treatment  and Quotas  9 ( Anti-Defamation  League
of B’nai  B'rith  pamphlet,  1974).

Indeed,  it appears  that,  if reverse  discrimination  is upheld
by the  Court,  any  institution  not  content  (as was  Fresno  State’s
School  of Social Work ) to make whites  the true minority  group
in  its  enrollment  could  simply  eliminate  them  altogether,  thus
opening  up the  maximum  number  of seats  to whatever  minority
groups  it wished,  in whatever  ratios  for  each  group  it thought
best.  Such  exclusion  of whites  might  be for one year or twenty,
or  until  such  minorities,  in the  school’s  unreviewable  judgment,

12

13

no  longer  needed  this  kind  of  aid.5  Nor  would  the  excluded
students  be  whites  only.  Again  taking  a  cue from  the  Fresno
State  approach,  a school  might  choose  to  eliminate  one  or  more
relatively  privileged  minority  groups  from  its  rolls  altogether,
thus  creating  even  more  room  for  members  of  worse-off
Fresno  State  has  actually  pointed  the way,  since
minorities.
Asians  were  consigned—along  with  Native  Americans—to the
10%  sub-quota  of the  class  reserved  for  “other  minonites.  By
shutting  them  out  entirely,  denying  them  even  the fractional
share  of a  sub-quota  that  they  originally  had,  additional  space
could  be  gained  for  minorities  “more  disadvantaged”  (in  the
school’s  eyes).

It might  be  argued  that  such  an  eventuality  is unlikely,  but
we  document  in  Point  I-B  actual  instances  of  reverse  dis-
crimination  against  the  very  groups  that  usually  benefit  from  it.

The  double  standard  adopted  by Petitioners  also  explains
why  their  Brief  so  casually  dismisses,  at  56-7,  Bakke’s  blasted
hopes  of a  medical  career,  and  why,  at  43  n.53,  it  calls  their
scheme  an  “experiment”  that  should  be  allowed  to  continue.
But  what  if blacks,  or  Chicanos,  or  other  minorities  classified  by
race  were  the  victims,  rather  than  Bakke  and  those  similarly
situated?

.

1%.

Petitioners  have  taken  the  wrong  road.  Civil  rights  were
obtained  only  when  whites  joined  and  worked  in  brotherhood
with  minority  groups,  enduring  much  suffering  along  the  road
to  civil  rights  progress.  Now  Petitioners  have  chosen  a  course
of action  that  sets  minorities  and  non-minorities  against  each
other,  creating  resentment  and  bitterness,  as  can  be  seen  even
from  the  plethora  of briefs  amicus  in the  instant  case.

Repeatedly,  Petitioners  and  the  Amici  supporting  them
characterize  the  program  under  review  here  as  designed  to
insure  the  inclusion  of minority  group  members  in the  medical
profession.  That,  of course,  is not  the  issue.  Rather,  this  case
hinges  on  whether  a  person  can  be  excluded  from  medical
school,  and  thus  from  the  profession,  solely  on  account  of his
race.

Because  of the  extremely  appealing  nature  of the  idea  of
including  more  blacks  and  other  minorities  in  medicine,  Peti-
tioners  stress  this  point  and  slough  over  the  excluding  of
persons  such  as  Respondent.  They attempt  to  build  their  entire
case  around a false  dichotomy,  trying  desperately  to  persuade
the Court  that  it must  choose  between  upholding  the challenged
program  and  inflicting  grievous  harm  on  the  nation’s  minority
population.  That  proposition  is wholly  without  merit.

Indeed,  as  we  will  demonstrate  more  fully in Part  I, infra,
the  challenged  program  is actually  harmful  to  minorities,  and
its abolition  is necessary  for their  sake  as  well  as  for the  sake  of
the  countless  more  obvious  victims  of reverse  discrimination  in
the  United  States  today  and  in the  future.

When  discrimination  agai..st  blacks  was  sanctioned  by the
law  of the  land,  it was  often  said  that  such  discrimination  was
really  for the good  of society.  Some,  for example,  even  asserted
that  the  Bible  and  God’s  eternal  plan  provided  for the  mainte-
nance  of blacks  in  a  subjugated  status:  civil  rights  proponents
were  thus  resisting  the  Divine  Will.  Others  contented  them-
selves  with  explaining  that  there  were  scientific  reasons  why
blacks  were  inferior  and  should  never  have  rights  equal  to  the
white  man’s.

14

1S

Nowadays,  similarly,  there  is  no  shortage  of  rationaliza-
tions  for  policies  of  reverse  discrimination.  We  are  told  that
they help the entire  society,  whites  as  well  as  blacks,  and  correct
(The  specific  ways  in  which  they  help  a
historical  wrongs.
Bakke  remain  unclear.)  But  the  primacy  of  the  Fourteenth
Amendment  right  to  be  free  from  discrimination—which  the
Supreme  Court  has  consistently  held  to  be  an  individually-  and
personally-  possessed  right,  not  a  right  belonging  to  any  racial
or  other  group  as  a  whole  —is simply  tossed  aside  and  forgotten
And  so  are  its
by  the  apologists  of  reverse  discrimination.
innocent  victims,  who  lose  their  chances  for  developing  their
talents,  engaging  in a  meaningful  career,  and  enriching  society.

Because  of the  widespread  imposition  of preferential  pol-
icies  in university  admissions  and  in public  and  private  employ-
ment,  it can  be  said  that  non-minority  persons  (and  also  men,
whenever  women  are  preferentially  hired  and  promoted,  or
admitted  to  institutions  of higher  learning),  are  disadvantaged,
along  with  members  of  the  historically  discriminated-against
groups  who  may  be  or  are  still  subjected  to  the  traditional
variety  of discrimination.  At  the  very  least,  however,  the  latter
victims  are  able  to  go  to  civil  rights  agencies  and  courts,  to
obtain  redress.  Those  who  suffer  from  reverse  discrimination,
though,  face  an  uphill  battle  because  the  discrimination  itself,
even  if conceded  to  have  taken  place,  is not  avowedly  illegal.

How  long  must  this  intolerable  situation  continue?  Cer-
tainly  it poisons  the  relationship  among  the faces  and  the ethnic
groups  that  comprise  our  diverse  population,  and  between  the
sexes.  The  recent  Gallup  poll  on  the  subject  shows  that  better
than  4  out  of  5  Americans  oppose  reverse  discrimination.
Among  non-white  respondents  to  the  poll,  sixty-four  percent
rejected  such  preferences.  New  York  Times,  May  1, 1977,  33.¢

is  somehow  constitutional  because  it

While  the proponents  of  reverse  discrimination  are  in
many  cases  trying  to  use  it (or,  at  least,  claim  the  intention  of
doing so) as a  mechanism  for solving  serious social  problems,
other methods exist  to cope  with  those  problems,  and  addition-
al solutions  can  also  be  developed  —although  perhaps  some  of
these soluuons  will  call  for certain  sacrifices from  the institutions
which  have been  engaging  in  reverse  discrimination,  rather
than  from  innocent  third  parties?’  We  submit  that  many
insututional  decision-makers  are  well  aware  of this  fact — which
is why they choose  to  resolve  their  conflict  of  interest  by
discnminaung  against non-minority  persons  and  males.  Often,
reverse discrimination  may  be  motivated  simply  by crass  self-
interest on  the part  of certain  holders  of institutional  power  who
believe  that they  can  “get  away  with”  blatant  disregard  of
human  rights  in order  to  raise  their  “body  count”  of particular
groups  and  thus  improve  their  public  relations  posture.

It must be remembered  that the medical  school  here is not
defending  some sort of program  requiring sacrifices  by the
school  itself  in the interest  of minorities,  such  as the estab-

.

Work  Education,  etc. This is all so obvious that it should

had  a life-or-death  contro!
over Bakke’s  chance  to be evaluated  without  racia! discrimination—and it is

16

17

Florida  College  of Medicine  at  Gainesville.  231  J.A.M.A.  1011
(1975).  Instead,  institutions  practicing  reverse  discrimination
expect  Respondent  and  countless  others  like  him,  to  give  up
their careers  in order to salve  the  school’s  collective  conscience
about  what  it is doing  for  minorities.  That,  we  submit, is  totally
unacceptable,  and  a  breach  of the  Constitution.

It is this  very  insistence  that  innocent  individuals  sacrifice
their  hopes  and  plans,  while  powerful  administrators  shirk  their
own  responsibilities,  that,  we  submit,  lends  an  especially  galling
quality  to  the  high-minded  sentiments  with  which  the  propo-
nents  of reverse  discrimination  characteristically  try to justify  it.
“The  most  odious  of all  oppressions  are  those  which  mask  as
justice.”  Jackson,  J.,  concurring,  Krulewitch  v.  United  States,
336  US.  440,  458  (1949).

Now  that  Congress,  as  part  of the  Education  Amendments
of  1976,  P.L.  94-482,  has,  in  20  USC  1232i(c),  barred  the
Secretary  of Health,  Education,  and  Welfare  from  forcing  racial
quotas  on  colleges,  universities,  and  professional  schools  as  a
condition  for  receiving  Federal  financial  aid,  the  Court,  it  is
hoped,  can  prevent  schools  from  doing  voluntarily  what  the
Secretary  could  not  make  them  do  involuntarily.

Just as it is simply  not permissible  to try to solve  a social  or

economic  problem  by  some  method  that  will  discriminate
against  minorities  (or  women),  and  just  as it is deemed
disrespectable  even  to suggest  such  a  course  of action,  exactly
the same  rule  must  govern  with respect  to alleged  “solutions”
those classed  as “non-
for  society’s  ills  that  discriminate  against
minorities”  —who,  ironically,  are  often  members  of  minority
ethnic  or religious  groups  themselves.

Indeed,  in many  cases,  the ancestors  of reverse  dis-
crimination’s  victims  were  not even in the United  States  at  the
time  of the prior  discrimination  against  blacks  and  other
minorities  which  is  used  as the excuse  for committing  reverse
discrimination  today.  Ironically,  many  of these  young  people,
especially  those  whose  forebears  came  over  somewhat  later

than the Mayflower  arrivals,  are themselves  members  of minor-
ity groups— although  the perpetrators  of the preferential  admis-
sions and employment  schemes  so in vogue  nowadays  doubtless
do not think  of persons  such  as It-‘o-Americans,
Catholics,
Polish-Amenicans,
Jews, and other groups  as being truly minor-
ites (though  numerically  by definition  they so qualify).  Ob-
viously,  i  erie  queens t  Geeies ca oo  Ge
instant  case  is reserved  only for members  of selected  minority
groups.

To  conclude  this Introduction,  we must  emphasize  the
heart of the problem:  that reverse  discrimination
focuses  on a
person's  group identification  rather  than on the individual,  his
unique talents  and his inalienable  nghts.  That,  of course, is the
basis of the very evils which it is supposedly  meant to  eliminate.
Until we learn  to look at  human beings  as individuals,  we will
never  have any hope  of eradicating  discrimination.

We therefore  respectfully  call to this Court's  attention  the
eloquent  words  of the United  States  Court  of Appeals  for the
Third  Circuit,  which,  in an employment  discrimination  case
involving  the Philadelphia  Police  Department,  expressed  a
Se  anne AGES CHGUEMY Ung GAGES Gee

the doors
from groups. Opening
viduals, as distinguished
long shut to minorities
is imperative,  but in so doing, we
must be careful  not to close  them  in the face of others,  lest
we abandon  the basic principle  of non-discrimination that
sparked  the effort to pry open those doors in the first

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

These  Amici  contend  that reverse  discrimination—a vari-
ant  of traditional  discrimination—is  not  constitutionally  per-
mussible as it relegates  traditional  merit concepts  of competition

and selection,  in the Bakke  context,  to the accidents  of skin
color,  race,  ethnicity  or gender,  none of  which allow  free and
open  competition  and  which  indeed,  if they  were  used  to
exclude  minorities,  would  be roundly  condemned by Petitioners
and their supporters.

while it may by its proponents  be
Reverse  discrimination,
urged  with  the  same  praiseworthy  intentions  as  was  Prohibi-
tion, nevertheless  like that errant  social  experiment  is flawed  by
the greater  long range  harm than good of which it is capable.
These  Amici,  while  not shocked,  are appalled  by the
position  taken by Petitioners  and their  supporters  with respect
to various  arguments  urged as supportive  of a license  to engage
in  blatant  and  outright  discrimination  against  non-minoniues.
Race is not a  bona  fide occupational  qualification,  and there  is
no compelling  state interest  to exclude  non-minorities  on the
basis of race.  Regardless  of the attempts  made  to belittle  the
contention  that reverse  discrimination  stigmatizes  its intended
beneficiaries,  it is irrefutably  documented  that  this occurs,  as
well  as the polarization  of the races  which  comprise  this
nation’s  population.

POINT  ONE:  EVEN  IF  IT  WERE  LEGAL,  REVERSE
DISCRIMINATION  WOULD  BE  AN  ILLUSORY  RE-
MEDY,  AN  UNSOUND  POLICY  AND  GROSSLY  UNJUST
TO  ALL  AMERICANS

A.  Bakke  Js  Indeed  A  Victim  Of Discrimination

The perpetrators  of reverse  discrimination  make  light  of
their victims’ suffering  and even have the temerity  to deny that
they are victims.  For instance,  Petitioners’  Brief,  at  72, alleges
that “the injury to respondent  is a  mere isolated influence  in his
life” and will not make either  him or others  feel that he is
“innately  inferior.”  That  is  arrant  nonsense.
This  “isolated
instance”  is merely  costing  him his whole  career,  as was
eloquently  stated  in a  letter to the New York  Times

“One  of the more noble concepts  of a  democratic  society is
that each shall be judged  on worth,  or effort, or merit, not
because  of race, or the color of one’s eyes, or the shape of
one’s  feet.
If  the Supreme  Court should  reject  Allan
Bakke’s  plea for admission  to a  medical  school  it would be
solely  because  Bakke is the wrong race.

Somewhere,  if this approach  is played to its final end, the
action of the  Supreme  Court would mean that a  single
minority individual,  not a  minority  race,  would be granted
admission to the school,  while a single  human being,  Allan
Bakke,  not an abstract  something  called “white society,”
would be denied admission.
If this occurs,  who then shall speak  for the human  being
known  as Allan  Bakke?  Who shall mourn  his shattered  life
and blocked  goals?  Where,  then, is the justice?”

I.  Herbert  Gordon,  Letter  to the Editor,  New York Ti
July 3, 1977, § 4, at 13.
=
Can anyone  imagine  even the most callous  perpetrator  of
“tradiuonal”  discrimination  against,  say,  women  or  blacks  thus

_

20

21

attempting,  in a  brief filed  in this  Court,  to  trivialize  the  harm  it
had  done?  Obviously  not,  but  then  the  issue  could  never  arise
now,  inasmuch  as  “traditional”  discrimination  against  those
groups  is irrevocably  recognized  as  illegal.  That,  however,  is
not  the  case  with  the  kind  of discrimination  committed  against
respondent,  who,  according  to  Petitioners,  is “innately  inferior”
in the  eyes  of the  law  because  he does  not  belong  to  one  of the
so-called  protected  groups.®

This  startling  conclusion—that  whites  are  denied  the
protection  of the  Equal  Protection  Clause,  let alone  the  benefits
of the  strict  scrutiny  test  is supposedly  justified  by one  of the
several  semantic  games  Petitioners  play—their  restrictive  defi-
nition  of the  word  “invidious,”  Jd.  at  72  and  73-4  n.72.  They
actually  allege  that  the  only  kind  of  racial  discrimination
outlawed  by that  Clause  is the type  bottomed  in notions  that  the
victim’s  group  is racially  inferior,  and  that  will  arguably  make
the  victim  feel  that  he  is  a  person  of lower  qualitative  value
solely  because  of his race.  Jd.  at  71-2.  This  self-serving,  twisted
version  of one  rationale  for  some  of the  Court’s  decisions  tries
to  transform  a  good  and  sound  reason  for  protecting  certain
groups,  such  as  blacks,  into  an  excuse  for  allowing  rampant,
unchecked  official  discrimination  against  members  of the  other
groups.®

In  the  light  of  the  specific  facts  herein,  it  is  difficult  to
understand  the  reasoning  relied  on  in Petitioners’  Brief at  56-7
which  alleges  that  the  injury  to  Bakke

,

“isa  diminished opportunity  to obtain  a benefit  that  he has
not previously enjoyed;  the  state  has  not  taken  away  from
him  a  position  already  achieved,  nor  has  it denied  him  an
advancement  to  which,  in  the  absence  of  a  remedial
scheme,  he could  otherwise  show  clear  entitlement.”
That  is obviously  a  misstatement,  because  in  fact  the  medical
school has previously  conceded  that  Bakke’s  credentials  were  so
high, and  that  even  the  “non-cognitive”  aspects  of his  creden-
tials—which  the  medical  school  terms  of  such  great  impor-
tance —were  also  sufficiently  high,  Brief  for  Respondent  in
Opposition  to Certiorari,  6-8, to  have  made  it impossible  for the
medical  school  to prove  that  he would  not  have  gotten  in absent
the  reverse  discrimination.  Petitioners’  Brief,  7-8.

In other  words,  but  for the  reverse  discrimination,  as  far as
one  can  tell, Bakke  would  have  been  admitted.  Even A cursory
glance at  his scores  compared  to those  of the specially  admitted
minority  students  shows  that  if he  were  judged  by the  same
standards  as  were  applied  to  them,  his  admission  would  have
been  a  foregone  conclusion.  Of course,  it must  be  noted  that
Bakke  measured  up  quite  well  even  among  the  regularly
admitted  students,  as  is graphically  demonstrated  by the  chart
in  Brief for  Respondent  in Opposition  to  Certiorari,  at  11.10

only slightly  better  qualified  than  the  admitted  minorities.  He is

Actually,  of  course,  it  is  doubtful  that  very  many  borderline
whites  are  now  admitted  to  medical  schools,  in  view  of  the

well-qualified  minority  group  members  whose  groups  are  not
included  in  the  quota  constellations  of certain  schools  to  which
they  apply.  (Examples  of the  latter  would  include  Japanese-
and  Chinese-Americans  in  two  of  the  three  actual  quota
schemes  set  forth  in  Brief  for  Respondent  in  Opposition  to
Certiorari,  30  n.13.)

However,  even  “borderline”  candidates  of any  race  should
not  be  subjected  to  racial  discrimination.  No  American  should
have  to  endure  that  evil  any  longer.  If they  are  to  be  rejected,
let  it be  done  on  the  merits,  not  on  a  racial  basis.

In  still  another  effort  to  negate  or  at  least  trivialize  the
harm  done  to  respondent,  Petitioners  characterize  the  personal
injury  to  him  as  no  different

—— + ee

“from  the  case  of a  family  displaced  from  its  home  by an
urban  renewal  project,  or  of  an  urban  applicant  whose
chances  for  admission  are  reduced  by an  admissions  policy
aimed  at  increasing  the  number  of doctors  likely to  practice
in  remote  areas.”

Petitioners’  Brief,  56.

Both  of  their  analogies  are  flagrantly  false.

A  family
displaced  from  its  home  by an  urban  renewal  project  must  not
be discriminated  against  by reason  of race,  and  such  a  family,  if
black,  must  of course  receive  the  same  kind  of relocation  aid

that  would  be  given  to  white  families.
Norwalk  CORE  v.
Norwalk  Redevelopment  Agency,  395  F.  2d  920  (2d  Cir.  1968).

It  would,  of  course,  be  unthinkable  to  suggest  that  the
victims  of such  displacement  be selected  on  racial  grounds.  But
this,  of course,  is what  the  university  has  done  to  Bakke.

acti
4 * ~~, Qa sencten

As  for  Petitioners’  analogy  to  selection  of applicants  who
appear  oriented  toward  an  eventual  rural  practice,  that  policy
can  and  should  be  administered  on  a  non-racial  basis.

B.  Anyone  Can  Become  a  Victim  of Reverse  Discrimination

It  must  be  emphasized  that  a  widespread  myth  about
reverse  discrimination  is untrue:  contrary  to  popular  belief,  the
direct  victims  of this  phenomenon  are  not  limited  to  the class  of
white  males.  In  some  cases,  members  of one  group  which  has
historically  been  a  target  for  discrimination  are  discriminated
against  in the  name  of helping  members  of another  such  group.
For example,  a New  York  Court  has  upheld  the  New  York  City
Commission  on  Human  Rights’  finding  in  favor  of  a  black
complainant  who  had  been  discriminated  against  because  the
local  school  board  wanted  to  award  the  position  in question—a
deputy  superintendency—to  an  Hispanic  applicant.  In  Re  City
Commission  on  Human  Rights  (Community  School  Board  No.
4),  No.  40523/75  (Supreme  Court,  N.Y.  County,  1975);  N-Y.
Post,  July  2,  1975,  at  11.

Conversely,  in  the  California  state  higher  education  sys-
tem,  several  Mexican-Americans  have a federal  class  action  suit
currently  pending  against  a state-run  college  for favoring  blacks
over  them  in employment  and  educational  programs.  Crofts  v.
Board  of Governors  of California  County  Colleges,  Civil  No.  75-
337  (N.D.  Cal.,  filed  Feb.  18,  1975);  Los  Angeles  Herald
Examiner,  Feb.  20,  1975,  at  A-7.  Plaintiffs  therein,  ironically,
are  represented  by the  Mexican-American  Legal  Defense  Fund,
which  has  also  filed  a  Brief  Amicus  supporting  the  Regents  in
Evidently  the  Fund—despite  the  pending
the  instant  case.
suit—has  no  objection  to  discrimination  favoring  blacks  if
Mexican-Americans  are  preferred  also.

96452  (1975)  and

the other  hand,  a  black  man filed  a  charge  with  the Minnesota
State  Department  of Human  Rights,  claiming  that  University  of

which,  they said,  was  reserved  for female  applicants  only.  B. R.

Thus,  for example,  in Hupart  vy.  Board of Higher Education
of the City  of New  York,  420  F.  Supp.  1087  (S.D.N.Y.  1976),
Asian-Americans  were  subjected  to  reverse  discrimination  in
choosing  entrants  to  a  six-year  combined  college  and  medical
school  program.
Jd,  1103.  This,  of course,  is only  to be
expected,  inasmuch  as what  Petitioners  are propounding  here is
that  all state  university  admissions  officials  be given  carte
blanche  to discriminate  in favor  of whichever  minorities  they
please,  and  against  whatever  non-minorities  and minorities  they
please.  Thus,  no one is really  safe  from  being  a  victim  of
reverse  discrimination.

To  further  show  the  arbitrary  nature  of  the decision  as to
which  groups  are to be included  and  which  to be excluded  in
“special  admissions,”  it  should  be  noted  that  Boalt  Hall,  the
University  of California  at  Berkeley’s  law  school,  has  stopped
preferenua!l  admission  of  Japanese-Americans,  despite  ample
pnor  discrimination  against  them,  including  the  interning  of
Amenican  citizens  of Japanese  descent  in  concentration  camps
in California  during  World  War  Il.  Furthermore,

“Currently,  the orientals  in California,  roughly  one  percent
of the population,  comprise  in some  instances  30 percent  of
the enrollment  in certain  engineering  and technical  schools.
Were  a  quota  system  to be introduced  in those  schools  in
order  to  favor  black  and  Mexican-American  applicants,  the
first losers  would  be applicants  from  the disproportionately
represented  oriental  group.”

M.  Fleming,  “The  Black  Quota  at  Yale  Law  School,”
[letter  to  Dean  Louis  H.  Pollak},  The  Public  Interest,  No.
19, Spring  1970,  44,  47.

26

27

It is submitted  that  the  foregoing  information  might  be of
keen  interest  to  two  groups  which  have  filed  briefs  amicus  in
this  case:  the  Asian-American  Bar  Association  of the  Greater
Bay  Area,  and  the  National  Fund  for  Minority  Engineering
Students.

This,  of course,  raises  a  question  obviously  not  limited  to
Asians.  For  example,  should  well-qualified  blacks  be  rejected
so  that  Native  American  students  can  gain  entry  to  a  school?
The  answer  must,  of course,  be  a  resounding  “no”!

In  conclusion,  we  wish  to  call  the  Court’s  attention  to  a
particularly  strong  statement  on  the  injustice,  in  view  of  the
many  minorities  who  compose  our  country,  of arbitrarily  selec-
ting  some  of  them  for ‘preferential  treatment.
Dr.  Morton
Teicher,  Dean  of the School  of Social  Work  at  the  University  of
North  Carolina,  Chapel  Hill,  was  writing  about  reverse  dis-
crimination  in  his  own  profession,  but  his  words  apply  to  all
sectors  of society:

“For  social  workers,  the  distinctions  among  people  based
on  race,  ethnic  backgre+ nd, religion,  or  creed  that  inhere  in
quota  systems  are  particularly  abhorrent.  Our  regard  for
the  individual  and  our  objection  to  hereditary  caste  as  a
status  determinant  should  make  quota  systems  especially
impossible  for  us  to  accept.

For  schools  of social  work,  the  argument  that  quotas  for
admission  will  produce  student  bodies  that  represent  the
proportion  of racial,  ethnic,  or  religious  groups  in society  is
a  curious  expression  of bigotry.  Proportional  representa-
tion  on  a  group  basis  is  highly  discriminatory.
What
taxonomy  shall  be  used  to  categorize  the  groups  that
should  be represented?  Among  the characteristics  that  defy
classification  are  the  following:  cultural,  economic,  ethnic,
gender,  geographic,  linguistic,  national,  occupational,  ra-
cial,  religious,  social  class,  and  tribal.

Some  minority  groups  include  the  following:  the  aged,
American  Indians,  Asian-Americans,  Blacks,  capitalists,
Catholics,  Chicanos,  easterners,  factory  workers,  farmers,
Hungarian-
German-Americans,
_Italian-
Americans,
Americans,  Jews,  the  lower  class,  migrants,  nomads,  north-
erners,  Polish-Americans,  the  poor,  Puerto  Ricans,  slum-
dwellers,  southerners,  Swedish-Americans,  the  upper  class,
WASPS,  westerners,  and  youths.

Hispanic  Americans,

 Irish-Americans,

immigrants,

Who  is  not  a  member  of a  minority  group?  Who  cannot
find  a  place  among  this  woefully  incomplete  list  of minor-
ities?

We  are  all  minorities.  Each  of us  comes  from  a  distinctive
racial,  religious,  or  ethnic  stock.  Each  of us  is  a newcomer
or  a  descendant  of  newcomers.
Even  the  native  Ameri-
cans—the  Indians—came  to  North  America  from  across
the  Bering  Straits  25,000  years  ago.  Each  of us  has  ties  to
our  own  roots.  Each  of us  has  pride  in our  origins.  Each
minority  contributes  to  America—to  its  building,  its evolu-
tion,  and  its  maintenance.”

Teicher,  “Reverse  Discrimination,”  Social  Work,

Nov.  1972,  3, 4.

C.  Reverse  Discrimination  Stigmatizes  Its  “Beneficiaries,”
Confirming  Rather  Than  Repudiating  Degoratory  Stereo-
types,  and  Inflicting  Serious  Harm  on  All  Members  of the
“Benefited”  Minority  Groups

In his  recent  cataloguing  of some  of the  arguments  against
reverse  discrimination,  Mr.  Justice  Brennan  noted  its  tendency
to  stigmatize  its  beneficiaries.
United  Jewish  Organizations  v.
Care,  — — US.  ~~,  51  L.Ed.  2d  229,  250-1  (1977)
(concurring  opinion ).

28

29

Among  the  many  writers  who  have  eloquently  articulated
their  dismay  concerning  this  particular  point  are  two  dis-
tinguished  black  civil  rights  leaders,  whose  analysis,  although  it
relates  to  employment,  is equally  true  with  respect  to  profes-
sional  school  admissions:

“There  are  those  who  argue  that,  on  a  short  term  basis,
quota  hiring  schemes  represent  an  effective  and  expedient
means  of resolving  a  difficult  social  problem.  But  we  are
convinced  that  the  inherent  dangers  of the  quota  principle
far  outweigh  any  temporary  gains  they  might  bring.

One  of the  most  serious  dangers  of the  quota  doctrine  is
that  it  will  perpetuate  the  stereotypical—and  profoundly
mistaken—view  that  blacks  lack  the  ability  and  the  will  to
Should  quotas  become  in-
make  it  on  their  own.
stitutionalized  as  government  policy,  society,  as  black
educator  Thomas  Sowell  has  warned,  would  no  doubt
conclude  that  Negroes  ‘must  be  given  something  in order
to  have  something.’

Quotas  would  further  entrench  the  tendency  of society  to
respond  to  the  call  for  equal  opportunity  with  tokenism.
Quotas,  in fact,  are  tokenism  taken  to its logical  conclusion.
Blacks  object  to  the  token  because  it  downgrades  the
dignity  and  abilities  of the  individual,  cheapening  both  his
or  her  accomplishments  and  the  accomplishments  of other
blacks  to  follow.  The  same  is true  of the  quota,  only  to  a
greater  degree.  The  black  who  benefits  from  the  quota
suffers  the  uncertainty  of never  knowing  whether  he  made
it on  his  merits,  or  was  simply  hired  to  meet  a government
decree.
As  for  the  dominant  white  society,  it  would
automatically  question  the  abilities  of all  blacks,  including
the  overwhelming  majority  who  have  succeeded  because
of their  intelligence,  skills,  and  self-discipline.”

Testimony  of Bayard  Rustin  and  Norman  Hill  (of the
A.  Phillip  Randolph  Institute),  Federal  Higher  Education
Civil
Programs—Institutional  Eligibility:  Hearings  on

Rights  Obligations  Before  the  Special  Subcommittee  on
Education  of  the  House  Committee  on  Education  and
Labor,  93d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.,  Part  2A,  at  253  (1974).

Other  prominent  voices  in the  black  community  have  been
raised  against  preferential  treatment,  for  these  very  reasons.
The  former  Executive  Director  of the  National  Association  for
the  Advancement  of Colored  People,  Roy  Wilkins,  has  warned
that  “|T]here  must  not  be  a  lowering  of standards.  Negroes
need  to insist on being  among  the  best, not on being  the best of
the  second-or-third-raters.”
Wilkins,  “The  Case  Against
Quotas,”  ADL  Bulletin,  March  1973,  at  4,  quoted  in  L.
Lavinsky,  “ DeFunis  v.  Odegaard:  The  ‘Non-Decision’  With  a
Message,”  75  Colum.  L.Rev.  520,  527  n.38  (1975).

Dr.  Thomas  Sowell,  an  economist,  who  is  also  black,
points  out  that  “reverse  discrimination”  is  a  failure  partly
because  “it  creates  the  impression  that  hard-won  achievements
of  these  groups  [minorities  and  women]  are  conferred  ben-
efits.”  Sowell,  “  ‘Affirmative  Action’  Reconsidered,”  The  Public
Interest,  Winter,  1976,  47,  at  63.
Several  years  earlier,  he
explained,  in  compelling  language,  the  reasoning  behind  his
view:

“What  all  the  arguments  and  campaigns  for  quotas  are
really  saying,  loud  and  clear,  is that  black people just don’t
have  it,  and that they  will  have  to be given  something in
order  to  have  something.  The  devastating  impact  of this
message  on  black  people—particularly  black  young  peo-
ple —will outweigh  any  few  extra  jobs that  may  result  from
this  strategy.  Those  black  people  who  are  already  com-
petent,  and  who  could  be  instrumental  in producing  more
competence  among  this  rising  generation,  will  be  com-
pletely  undermined,  as  black  becomes  synonymous —in  the
minds  of black  and  white  alike—with  incompetence,  and
black  achievement  becomes  synonymous  with  charity  or
payoffs.”

Sowell,  Black  Education:  Myths  and  Tragedies  292

(1972).

30

31

Petitioners’  Brief,  at  48,  alleges  that  “respondent  is plainly
in  no  position  to  assert”  the  claim  that  “the  Davis  program
threatens  to  depict  minorities  as  incapable  of  holding  their
own.”  That  statement  is  seriously  in  error.  Respondent  most
certainly  can  assert  this claim,  because  it directly  contradicts  the
aims  given  by Petitioners  as  a justification  for  this  program,  at
32-3  of their  Brief.  Stigmatization  will  obviously  work  against
what  Petitioners  hope  to  achieve  by means  of their  preferential
scheme.  By “stigmatization,”  of course,  we  mean  both  what  the
general  public  and  other  minority  persons  think  about  the
beneficiaries  of  such  programs,  and  what  these  beneficiaries
think  about  themselves.

Petitioners’  Brief,  at  48  n.55,  and  the  Brief  Amicus  of the
Association  of American  Law  Schools,  at  62,  both  point  to  the
Briefs  Amici  submitted  in  support  of Petitioners  by organiza-
tions  representing  minority  groups  as  proof  that  programs  of
preferential  treatment  in  admissions  do  not  stigmatize  minor-
ities.  If that  were  so,  it is argued,  why then  would  these  groups,
organized  to  protect  minorities,  approve  of such  programs?  We
can  only  respond  that,  while  obviously  certain  persons  in
positions  of power  within  various  minority-group  organizations
may  indeed  approve  of preferential  treatment,  the average  non-
white  American  does  not.  Whether  because  of the  stigmatizing
effects  of  reverse  discrimination,  or  for  other  reasons,  64%  of
non-white  Americans  reject  the  idea  of preferential  treatment,
27%  favor  it, and  9%  have  no  opinion.  (The  national  figures
for  all  Americans  are  83%  opposed,  10%  in favor,  and  7% with
no  opinion.)  Press  release,  The  Gallup  Poll,  May  1, 1977,  at  3,
summarized  in  New  York  Times,  May  1, 1977,  at  33.

Thus,  it would  seem  that  regardless  of what  opinions  might
be held by various  ideologues  of all races on this issue,  our non-
white  citizens  reject  preferential  treatment  by  a  substantial
margin,  indeed.
They  may  well  resent  bitterly  the  racist
implications  of such  programs,  since  it  is  inescapable  that  “a
racial  quota  is  derogatory  of  and  patronizing  to  the  intended

beneficiary  minority.”  Chmill  v.  City  of Pittsburgh,  No.  1047
15
C.D.  1976,  __.  Commonwealth  Ct.

monwealth  Ct., July 15,  1977).

D.  Evidence  Indicates  That  Preferential  Admissions  Leads  to

Lowered  Professional  Standards

Petitioners  keep  stressing  the  “compelling  state  interests”
supposedly  involved  here.  We  would  like,  however,  to  bring  up
a  different  kind  of notion—a  “compelling  patients’  interest”  to
have  the  best  possible  available  medical  care.

Whether  or  not  it is a  legal  right  in the  enforceable  rather
than  in some  inchoate  sense,  there  is doubtlessly  a  moral  right
It
of  every  patient  to  have  the  best  medical  care  available.
would  seem  that  every  American  would  possess  this  right  in
equal  quantity.  However,  it would  behoove  decision  makers
with  the power  of  selection  to try especially  to protect  the
exercise
from  disadvantaged
backgrounds.  Such  patients,  more  than  most  of us,  are  unable
to protect  this  interest  on their  own,  since  they must  rely  on
clinic  personnel  rather  than  pick  their  own  personal  physicians.

right  by  persons

of  this

We  submit  that  the  medical  school’s  policy  here  clearly
goes  against  the  average  Californians’  “compelling  patients’
interest”  in  receiving  high  quality  medical  care,  and  especially
violates  that  interest  in  regard  to  minority-group  and  other
disadvantaged  persons.

Medicine  is an art, not a  science,  and much  can go wrong
even  when  one  is  attended  by the  finest  physicians  available.
But  it behooves  the  decision  makers  who,  in effect,  choose  our
doctors  for us by deciding  who is to be admitted  to our medical
schools,  to take  only  the best-qualified  people,  chosen  by the
most  relevant  and  well-validated  criteria  possible.  Any  tam-
pering  with  academic  standards  exposes  the innocent  eventual
patients  of unqualified  graduates  of medical  schools  to  injury
and  death  as  a  result  of malpractice.

33

Indeed,  we  would  also  say  that  it amounts  to  a compelling
state  interest  in  traditional  terms  for  our  doctors  to  be  chosen
from  the  best  qualified  applicants.  That  could  be accomplished
by  the  Davis  medical  school  if  another  state  interest  were
If  the
recognized—that  of  ending  ail  racial  discrimination.
blatant  discrimination  now  carried  on  by  the  medical  school

advantaged  and  disadvantaged—could  be  assured  that  their
doctors  were  selected  from  the  best  applicants  available.

The  Brief Amicus  of the Antioch  School  of Law,  at  24 n.20,
cites  a  study  which  purports  to  show  that  minority  medical
students  preferentially  admitted  eventually  do as  well  in clinical
contexts  as  do  those  regularly  admitted.
H.  Simon  and  J.
Covell,  “Performances  of Medical  Students  Admitted  via  Regu-
lar  and  Admission-Variance  Routes,”  50  J.  Med.  Ed.  237
(1975).  Shortcomings  in that  research  were  pointed  out  by Dr.
Dante  G.  Scarpelli  of the  University  of Kansas  Medical  Center:

“The  averages  cited  in  this  study  include  the  scores  of
seven  minority  students  who  did  not  pass  the  National
Board  Examination.  Although  attitudinal  attributes,  basic
clinical  skills,  and  the  humanism  necessary  in  a  physician
are  crucial  and  important  measurements,  they  should  not
weigh  more  heavily  in  the  final  judgment  of competence
than  the  basic  fund  of science  necessary  to  practice  modern
scientific  medicine.  ..

.

...  Simon  and  Covell  point out  that  the  success  of minority
students  granted  ‘admission  variances’  is due  to a flexible
curriculum  that  permits  deceleration  of  progress  and
reduction  of  load  as  well  as  extensive  tutorial  and  other
I  question,  as  I  have  before,  whether  the
supports.
products  of decelerated  programs  are  capable  of attaining
competence  in  the  strict  sense  of the  word.
Is  a  student
who,  by  virtue  of  low  MCAT  scores  and  grade-point
average,  takes  only  one  course  at  a  time  in contrast  to  the
usual  two  or  three,  and  often  repeats  such  courses  two  or

three  times,  ever  capable  of achieving  true  competence?  It
seems  to  me  that  our  responsibility  as  medical  educators  is
to  produce  the  best  possible  professionals,  not  marginally
competent  or  incompetent  ones.  To  do  less  is  not  in  the
best  interests  of society  or  the  profession.”

Letter  to  the  Editor,  293  New  Engl.  J.  Med.  206,  207

(1975).12

The  Antioch  Brief Amicus,  at  26, also  mentions  an  incident
at  the  Harvard  Medical  School  “where  one  professor  attacked
minority  admissions  citing  the  example  of a  minority  student
who  had  failed  Part  I of the  National  Boards  five  times  and  yet
was  granted  his  M.D.  degree.”  This  is  incorrect.  The  actual
article,  which  is reprinted  as  an  appendix  infra,  does  not  attack
lower  admission  standards,  and,  at  the  beginning  of its  third
paragraph,  even  approves  of  them  “to  help  disadvantaged
groups.”  It really  deals  with  grading,  retention  and  graduation
criteria—and  warns  how  these  are  being  impaired  and  are
slipping  badly.
B.  Davis,  “Academic  Standards  in  Medical
Schools,”  294  New  Engl.  J.  Med.  1118-9  (1976).

Dr.  Halberstam  makes  clear,  however,  that  most  minority

Petitioners’  Brief presents  (at 45  n.54)  some  data  designed
to  show  that  minority  students  do  very  well  in  medical  school.
Unfortunately,  that  is simply  not  the  case.  The  latest  available
figures  show  a  disturbingly  high  rate  of failure  among  minority
students,  indicating  the  inevitable  results  of admitting  unquali-
fied  and  unprepared  applicants.
Incredibly,  not  only  the
percentage  but  even  the  absolute  number  of black  students  who
repeat  their  first  year  is  greater  than  that  for  non-minority
students,  according  to  the  latest  figures.  159  of the  1,052  black
students  repeated  their  first  year  compared  to  only  157  of the
13,808  non-minority  students.  Expressed  as  percentages,  15.1%
of  the  black  students  repeated  the  first  year,  as  compared  to
only  1.1%  of non-minority  students.  For  Puerto-Rican  students
the  figure  was  10.3%  repeating,  for Mexican-American  students
9.2%  and  for  American  Indian  students  6.6%.
“Medical
Education  in  the  United  States,  1975-1976:  Undergraduate
Medical  Education,”  236  J.A.M.A.  2957,  Table  15  at  2963
(1976).

Another  table  shows  that  of those  black  students  admitted
in the  1973-1974  class,  only  87%  were  retained  as  of June  1976.
For  non-minority  students,  the  figure  was  97%.  Although  more
recent  figures  are  somewhat  more  favorable  for  minority  stu-
dents,  it may  well  be that  the  schools  involved  simply  are  giving
the  less  qualified  of these  students  a somewhat  longer  chance  to
succeed  at  their  studies  before  dismissing  them—or  before  the
students  themselves  simply  give up and  drop out.  /d., Table  24
at  2964.

These  statistics  make  unpleasant  reading  indeed,  and  we
regret  the  need  to  mention  them,  but  doing  so  is essential  in
order  to  help  demonstrate  why  reverse  discrimination  must  be
eliminated.  Eradicating  it would  prevent:  (1)  further  harm  to
innocent  minority  students  used  as  pawns  by  medical  schools
callously  wishing  to  raise  their  “body  count”  of such  persons
regardless  of the  human  cost  and  (2)  the  inexcusable  waste  of
vital  medical  training  resources  stemming  from  “dropping  out”
and  dismissal  for  academic  reasons.

The  vaunted  benefits  of having  a “racially  diverse”  student
body  would,  it  would  seem,  evaporate  in  the  face  of  the
widespread  academic  failure  of preferentially  admitted  minor-
ity students.  Obviously,  it is a distinct  disservice  to  these  young
people  to  admit  them  to  schools  where  they  cannot  succeed,
and  where  their  poor  performance  confirms  rather  than  dispels
the  false  stereotypes  about  minority  abilities  which  are  no  doubt
shared  by  medical  students  and  medical  school  instructors
probably  to  the  same  extent  that  they  exist  in  other  strata  of
society.  And  one  can  only  guess  at  the  trauma  inflicted  on  the
failing  minority  students  themselves,  who  must  experience  the
shame  and  humiliation.  Such  statistics  do  not  seemingly  cause
much  concern  to  the  medical  school  administrators  who  keep
implementing  the  programs  of  preferential  admissions  which
If they  really  cared
inevitably  end  in  such  human  tragedies.
about  minority  students—and  if they  really  cared  about  the
other  victims  of preferential  treatment—the  applicants  who  are
denied  the  places  rightfully  theirs,  solely  because  of  their

race —these  unfortunate  events  would  not  occur.  But  of course,
the  basic  element  of racial  discrimination  is this  very  inability  to
see  people  as  human  beings  who  can  suffer  and  cry  and  bleed,
and  the  insistence  on  looking  at  them  as  faceless  members  of
groups,  all  of whose  members  are  presumed  to  possess  certain
characteristics  and  to  collectively  possess  rights  which  are  not
possessed  by an  individual  qua  individual.  A decision-making
process  which  considered  applicants  as  human  beings  individ-
ually  possessing  basic  human  rights  would  reject  outright  the
idea  of preferential  treatment.

However,  Petitioners,  in  the  guise  of  helping  “society,”
manage  to  hurt  both  minorities  and  non-minorities  simulta-
neously.

E.  There  are  Alternatives  to Preferential  Admissions  that  will
Accommodate  Disadvantaged  Applicants  of all  Groups

The  existence  of  a  special  “minorities-only”  admissions
program  puts  undue  focus  on  the  minority  status  of  such
persons,  making  it  appear  as  if  they  could  not  get  into
professional  schools  on  their own  merits,  and  that  their  minority
status  has  an  inherent  relation  to  that  fact.  Instead  of over-
coming  the  unfortunate  stereotypes  about  minorities  being
inferior  to  non-minorities,  such  a scheme  only accentuates  them
and  gives  these  canards  more  credence.

Although,  as  we  documented  in  the  Introduction,  most
Americans  reject  reverse  discrimination,  these  Amici  are  certain
that the overwhelming  majority  of our  citizens  believe  in giving
a  fair chance  to every  person,  regardless  of group  identification,
who  has  been—through  no  fault  of his own—the  victim  of past
deprivation.  Any  non-discriminatory  efforts  to  accomplish  that
end (without  lowering  academic  standards)  would,  we  think,
win  popular  support,  and  eliminate  the  stigma  that  would
otherwise  attach  to  the  minority  graduates  thus  admitted.  Such
minority  alumni  would  be  seen  as  no  different  from  the  non-
minority  beneficiaries  of such  a  program.

38

39

J.  H.  Bunzel,  “Bakke  vs.  University  of  California,”

Commentary,  March  1977,  59,  64.

As  a  well  known  black  professor  of labor  law  puts  it,

“In  one  year  here,  {Harry  T.]  Edwards  has  observed  that
blacks  at  the  law  school  feel  ‘alienated’  because  ‘their
qualifications  are  still  being  questioned’  by  alumni  and
employers.  To  quash  challenges  to  the  incompetence  of
minority  students  and  graduates,  the  law  school  ‘must
insure  that  blacks,  women,  and  other  minorities  come  in on
an  equal  basis.”  ”

Harvard  Law  Record,  September  24,  1976,  at  6.
Certainly  the  value  of  having  more  racial  and  ethnic
minorities  in  a  professional  school,  to  interact  with  other
students—a  situation  which  petitioners  believe  so  desirable  that
it  amounts  to  a  compelling  state  interest—is  markedly  de-
creased,  if not  destroyed,  by the  knowledge  on  the  part  of all
students  that  these  minorities  were  preferentially  admitted.

While  preferential  treatment  should  not  be  extended  on
grounds  of socio-economic  disadvantage  (even  if this  could  be
done  on  a  non-racial  basis),  there  is nothing  wrong  in  taking
into  account,  when  evaluating  the  credentials  of  a  particular
candidate,  that  he  or  she  comes  from  a  socio-economically
disadvantaged  background.  Of course,  it must  not  be assumed
that  all  the  members  of any  particular  group  come  from  such
background,  since  that  is obviously  not  so.

In  defense  of  a  disadvantagement  approach  to  medical
school  admissions  for  minorities  and  other  deserving  students
who  have  not  gotten  the  proper  background,  one  physician
points  out  this  obvious  injustice  in existing  programs:

“At  present,  even  when  the  black  applicant’s  family  is
middle-class,  professional,  and  not  deprived,  that  applicant
gets  preference  over  a  white  applicant  of less  affluent  and
privileged  background.  Under  circumstances  in which  an
academically  better  but  poverty-level  white  competes  for

admission  with  a  less  qualified,  affluent  black,  under
.
affirmative  action  the  black  gets  admitted.  Is this  fair? . .

In recognizing that  much  can be said  for special procedures
for  deserving  students  of deprived  backgrounds,  I think  it
far better  to  base  these  programs  on  economic  need,  rather
than  on  racial,  ethnic,  or  sex  determinants.  If economics
were  the  touchstone,  in  fact,  many  blacks  would  get
preference,  but  so  would  some  equally  deserving  under-
privileged  whites.  Furthermore,  no  constitutional  proscrip-
tions  vould  be  violated,  no  quotas  would  be  defined,  no
undeserving  students  would  be  carried  for  the  sake  of  a
false  premise,  and  no  new  mythology  would  be created.”

A.  L.  Flick,  Letter  to  the  Editor,  293  New  Engl.  J.

Med.  6 (1975).

While  these  Amici  do  not  agree  that  preferential  treatment
for  the  disadvantaged  is the  answer,  it would  certainly  be  an
improvement  over  the  present  policies.  We  believe  that  rather
than  have  supposedly  non-discriminatory  quotas  for  the  dis-
advantaged,  individuals  with  claimed  backgrounds  of  past
deprivation  should  first  be  given  remedial  assistance  in  devel-
oping  their  capacities  to  their  fullest,  so  that  they  may  compete
successfully  in the  regular  admissions  process.

Petitioners  and  their  supporters  urge  that  there  is need  (i.e.
there  is  no  workable  alternative)  to  resort  to  the  sort  of
preferential  scheme  as  employed  by  the  medical  school  at
Davis—and  at  other  professional  schools,  but  this  is myth.

For  example,  other  professional  schools,  quietly  and  with-
out  fanfare,  can  and  have  significantly  achieved  an  increase
(numenically  and  percentage-wise ) in minorities  enrolled  with-
Out resort  to  quotas,  goals  or  other  devices  for  excluding  non-
minorities  by  employing  pre-admission  remedial  education
programs.

The  United  States  Naval  Academy,  has  raised,  between
the  1976  and  1980  entering  classes,  the  numbers  of minorities

40

41

(“blacks”  and  “ethnics”)  as  is  demonstrated  in  one  of  the
Appendices  attached  (a  copy  of  a  letter  of  the  Executive
Director  of the  U.S.  Naval  Academy  Foundation)  by a  process
of  upgrading  disadvantaged  potential  candidates  so  that  they
might  qualify  under  the  Academy’s  traditional  standards  and
be  equipped  to  compete  with  other  non-“disadvantaged”  Aca-
demy  students.  "4

Specifically,  the  minorities  entering  rose  from  167  to  222
between  the  1976  and  1980  classes  (an  increase  from  12.5%  to
17.1%  of  total  enrollment)  during  which  period  the  entering
class  declined  from  1334  to  1229.

Although  an  undergraduate  institution,  the  Naval  Aca-
demy  is actually  a  professional  school,  with  strong  emphasis  on
the  exact  sciences,  and  looks  for  certain  unusual  personal
Thus  its  experience  is  a  valid
qualities  in  its  applicants.
precedent  for what  might  be done  by medical  schools.  See  also,
for  a  proposal  of  “pre-college  training  centers,”  T.  Sowell,
Black  Education:  Myths  and  Tragedies  308-10  (1972),  and
Chapters  III  and  IV,  dealing  respectively  with  high  school  and
college  programs  for  would-be  black  physicians,  in J.L.  Curtis,
Blacks,  Medical  Schools,  and  Society  (1971).

Such  programs  in  medical  school  will  be  facilitated  by the
Health  Professions  Educational  Assistance  Act  of  1976,  P.  L.
94-484.  See  especially  the  programming  described  in  42  USC
§ 295g-7,  which  precisely  meets  these  needs for medical  schools.
Congress  has  authorized  $20,000,000 for each  of the  next  three
fiscal years for these purposes.  Id.

students  from  low-income  families  had  not  been  successfully
recruited  by the  medical  schools,  whereas  black  students  from

Petitioners’  Brief,  at  38,  alleges  that  “[A]  truly  racially
‘neutral’  disadvantaged  approach  would  do  little  more  than
substitute  less-affluent  whites  for  more-affluent  whites.  What-
ever  the  desirability  of such  a  result,  it would  not  achieve  the
goal  of significant  minority  participation.”(  footnote  omitted ).
This  overlooks  the  fact  that  large  numbers  of disadvantaged
whites  from  various  ethnic  groups  and  in rural  poverty  pockets
scattered  throughout  our  country  deserve  physicians  to  take
care  of  them,  and  if  Petitioners  hypothesize  correctly  that
“specially  admitted”  students  will  return  to  indigenous  areas
once  they are  graduated,  and  practice  there,  then  Petitioners  are
denying  these  poor  whites  medical  help which  they  badly  need.
Perhaps  it is  not  as  “trendy”  to  help  white  ethnics  and  poor
rural  whites  as  it is to  help other  minorities.  Once  again,  we  see
Petitioners’  double  standard  in action.

With  respect  to  law  school  admissions,  the  point  has  been
made  that  if  preferential  treatment  were  abolished,  many
minority  applicants  who  would  then  be  turned  down  by  the
highly  selective  law  schools  would  still  be  eligible  to  be
admitted  on  a  merit  basis  to  the  less  selective  schools.  Dean  of
New  York  Law  School  E.  Donald  Shapiro  believes  “that
perhaps  his school  would  start  getting  minority  applicants,  who,
under  a  special  admission  program,  would  ordinarily  have
gotten  into  Harvard  or  Yale.”  N. Y.L.J.,  February  25,  1977,  p.1,
at  2.  Another  writer  has  elaborated  on  this  point:

“There  are  many  good  regional  and  local  law  schools  in
Philadelphia,  Boston,  Los  Angeles  and  other  metropolitan
areas,  where  black  law  students  can  compete  with  white
law students  on  equal  terms  and  where  they can  study  law
in  competition  with  students  of similar  qualifications  and
aptitudes.  Many  of these  law  schools  do not  follow  Yale’s
policy  of numerically  limited  enrollment  and  are  geared  to

42

43

handle  within  reason  all  students  who  can  qualify  for
admission.  In view  of the  prevalence  of these  law  schools,
the  relative  ease  of admission  to  many  of them,  and  their
flexibility  in  handling  increased  numbers  of students,  the
initiation  of  a  system  of  proportional  representation  for
black  students  at  Yale  Law  School  serves  no  genuine  need
or  purpose.”

M.  Fleming,  “The  Black  Quota  at  Yale  Law  School”

A  recent  contribution  to  the  psychological  literature  sheds
some  light  on  the  issues  in  this  case.
It  shows  that,  psy-
chometrically,  it is possible  to  measure  individual  disadvantage,
and  also  to  predict  the  possible  utility  to  society  of educating
that  person.

This  could  be  done  on  a  non-racial  basis.

F.  Petitioners  Fail  to  Grasp  the  True  Meaning  of Merit  and

Why  it Should  Preserved

Petitioners’  Brief  makes  numerous  mistakes  in  its  dis-
cussion  of  the  “merit  system”  in  medical  school  admissions.
For  example,  at  31-2  it  confuses  a  legitimate  idea—the  in-
clusion  of  reliance  on  appropriate  non-cognitive  factors  in
addition  to  science  proficiency—with  something  grossly  illegiti-
mate:  racial  discrimination  in  favor  of minority  applicants.

At  50-51,  Petitioners  criticize  the  “premises  underlying  the
.  .” Id.
plea to  meritocracy  in opposition  to the  Davis  Program.  .
What  they  forget  is  that  if  the  normal  admissions
at  50.
process,  run  on  merit  principles,  does  not  in fact  select  the  best
doctors,  it is  obviously  keeping  out  good  white  applicants  as
Therefore,  it should  be  changed.  But
well  as  minority  ones.
this  is obviously  no  excuse  for  discrimination.  Respondent  is
not  attempting  to  force  false  assumptions  as  to  educational

policy on  Petitioners,  as  charged  at  51.  Rather,  he is only trying
to  enforce  Petitioners’  legal  obligation  not  to  discriminate
racially  against  him.  While  faculty judgments  that  supplement
“numerical  indicators”  are  permissible,  id.  51-2,  Respondent
more  than  satisfied  those  in  every  respect.
He  was  rejected
nonetheless,  though,  because  his  skin  was  the  wrong  color.

Furthermore,  with  respect  to  law  school  admissions,  the
numerical  indicators,  “if anything,  overpredict  the  performance
in  law  school  of blacks  and  Mexican-Americans.”  Law  School
Deans’  Brief  on  Certiorari,  18  n.5;  L.  Lavinsky,  “DeFunis  v.
Odegaard:  The  ‘Non-Decision’  With  a  Message,”  75  Colum.  L.
Rev.  520,  531-2,  n.70.  (1975).  These  Amici,  however,  do  not
wish  to  defend  the  existing  standardized  tests  for  either  law
school  or  medical  school admissions.  We say only this: if the
tests are not valid in predicting  who will be the best attorneys or
physicians,  let  them  be  set  aside,  or  at  least  discounted  with
respect  to  their  relative  weight,  and  let  better  measures  be
developed.

But  protests  against  the  existing  standardized  tests  or  other
forms  of  measurements,  however  justified,  do  not  mean  that
objective  standards  can  simply  be dropped,  and  discriminatory
selection  instituted.  '5

44

The  same  considerations  apply  in  regard  to  admissions  as
apply  to  a  related  subject—employment.  It has  been held that
neither  the  mere  reluctance  of minorities  to  apply  for jobs  from
which  they  have  historically  been  excluded,  nor  the  mere  fact
that  existing  screening  and  testing  measures  have  a  dis-
criminatory  impact  on  actually  qualified job applicants,  justifies
preferential  treatment  in  employment.
Rather,  recruiting  of
applicants  to  enlarge  the  pool,  and  the  development  of non-
discriminatory  testing  must  be pursued.  State  Dept.  of Adminis-
tration  v.  Dept.  of Industry,  Labor  & Human  Relations,
Wisc.2d  —__,  252  N.W.  2d  353,  358  (1977).

Petitioners  misstate  the  issue  in  their  Brief,  at  53,  when
they  assert  that  universities  must  be  able  to  “serve  other  goals”
in  addition  to  merit.  (They  refuse  to  concede  the  fact  that  it is
merely  racial  discrimination  that  the  law  relevant  to  this  case
forecloses  as  a  device  to  reach  ends  deemed  socially  useful;
other  forms  of criteria  for  selection  of,  say,  medical  students
may  still  be  permissible,  and  indeed  are. )

The  three  examples  picked  by  Petitioners  to  serve  as
examples  of the  “other  goals”  are  indeed  unfortunate  choices
Jd,  53.  The  question  of  civil
from  Petitioners’  standpoint.
service  employment  preferences  for  veterans  has  come  under
vigorous  litigative  attack  as  being  discriminatory  against  wom-
en,  to whom  opportunities  to serve  in the armed  forces  have
been  relatively  foreclosed.
Efforts  to  recruit  medical  students
likely  to  practice  in  rural  areas,  although  enurely legitimate,
must  be  implemented  on  a  non-racially  discriminatory  basis.
As  for Morton  v.  Mancari,  that  decision  and  its relevance  to  the
instant  case  are discussed  infra;  suffice  it to say here  that the
statutory  hiring  preference  enacted  by Congress  and  upheld by
the  Court  in Morton  gives  no  comfort  to  Petitioners  herein.

notional culture, and pay the peice for the ideslogical  whims of whises

JR.  Silber,  “The  Need  for Elite Education,”  Harper's,  June  1977,

22,  24.

Respondent  asks  this  Court  not  to  “posit  a  particular  view
of the  merit  principle  and  then  to  mandate  exclusive  adherence
to  it by independent  entities  commissioned  by the  citizenry  to
make  decisions  on  matters  of social  policy  of abundant  com-
plexity  and  minimal  susceptibility  to  fixed  answers.”  Id.,  at  54.
He  merely  asks the Court to rule again, as it  has so many times
on  behalf of non-whites,  that  one’s  quantum  of merit,  and  one’s
right  to be free  from discrimination,  does not in any way
depend  on  one’s  race.
Petitioners  simply  fail  to  acknowledge
that  merit  is invariably  raised  by Respondent  and  all  the Amici
who  support  him,  not  in its own  right  but in the  context  of non-
discrimination—a  context  which  of course  Petitioners  insist  on
ignoring.

Certain  briefs  submitted  in this  case  allege  the  existence  of
various  abuses  and  violations  of the  merit  concept  in higher and
professional  education  admissions,  arguing  that  such  lapses
from  the  merit  standard  justify  preferences  for  minorities.  But
the obvious  answer  to this line of attack  is that—while  they may
not  contravene  the  Equal  Protection  Clause—non-merit  admis-
sions  based  on  improper  criteria  should  be  banished  from
academe.

As an  example  of how  such  practices  can  be eliminated,  we
call  attention  to  a  1975  incident  at  the  University  of  South
Carolina  Law  Center,  where  a protest  by the faculty  resulted  in
the forced  withdrawal
of eleven students
who had been denied
admission  to the school but had then been allowed  to  enroll  under
a waiver  granted by the  University’s  board of trustees.  Several  of
the  students  were  offspring  of leading  figures  in South  Carolina
politics.  Columbia,  S.C.  State,  Oct.  23,  1975,  1-A;  Id., Oct.  24,
1975,  1-D.

Ironically,  in that  case,  an inspection  team  from  the
American
Bar Association,  after an on-site  visit,  had warned
that  these  “non-merit”  admissions  jeopardized  the  school’s
ABA  accreditation.  However,  the ABA  (evidently  comfortable
with  a double  standard ) has  nothing  but  praise  for preferential

46

47

admissions  of  minority  students,  as  evidenced  by  its  Brief
Amicus  filed  in  the  instant  case—despite  the  fact  that  those
programs,  too,  involve  a  waiver  of  normal  admissions  stan-
dards.

In  conclusion,  these  Amici  wish  to  emphasize  that  surely
the  great  increases  in  the  numbers  of minority  students  now
attending  college  will  supply  a  large  enough  pool  of  well-
qualified  minority  candidates  for  medical  and  other  profes-
sional  schools.
In  fact,  the  number  of  black  college  students
under  35  has  more  than  doubled  since  1970,  from  0.5  million  to
1.1  million  (in  1976).  Blacks,  in  1976,  comprised  11.7%  of the
civilian  population  between  the  ages  of  16  and  34,  and  black
students  were  10.7%  of all  college  students  in  that  age  range,
showing  that  the  gap  between  the  races  is rapidly  narrowing.
In  fact,  while  76.7%  of the  black  collegians  were  attending  full
time,  only  71.4%  of the  whites  were.  U.S.  Dept.  of Commerce
(Bureau  of  the  Census),  Current  Population  Reports:  Popu-
lation  Characteristics—School  Enrollment—Social  and  Eco-
nomic  Characteristics  of  Students:  October  1976  (Advance
Report ), Series  P-20,  No.  309  (July  1977),  2, 5.

Perhaps  the  reason  that  Petitioners  and  certain  Amici
supporting  them  find  so  few  minority  applicants  who  satisfy  the
“numerical  indicators”  is that  many  of the  most  well-qualified
black  students  are  being  held  back,  denied  college  admission
and  financial  aid  because—incredibly—they  are  perceived,  by
black  and  white  decision-makers  alike,  as  non-needy  and
“middle  class”  instead  of “authentic  ghetto  types.”  For  thor-
ough  documentation  of the  existence  of this  bizarre  dichotomy,
see  T.  Sowell,  Black  Education:  Myths  and  Tragedies,  Chapters
5 and  6 (1972).  (One  reason  for  the  rejection  of  the “middle
class”  blacks  might  be  that  the  “ghetto  types”  will  lend  more
“diversity”  to  campuses,  inasmuch  as  that  is  such  a  valued
quality,  according  to  Petitioners  and  such  Amici  as  the  Ameri-
can  Association  of University  Professors. )

G.  Obtaining  “Diversity”  in a  Student  Body  Does  Not  Justify

Reverse  Discrimination

One  of  the  prime  excuses  alleged  by  Petitioners  as
justifying  their  quota  scheme  is  that  increased  numbers  of
minority  students  in  the  medical  school  will  provide  a  see-
mingly  priceless  quality  called  “diversity.”  E.g.,  Petitioners’
Brief,  51.  Indeed,  the  Brief  Amicus  of the  American  Associ-
ation  of University  Professors  is entirely  devoted  to  this  propo-
sition.

These  Amici  agree  that  diversity  in  a  student  body  can  be
beneficial  and  should  be  encouraged.  But  doing  so  seems  an
incredibly  flimsy  reason  to  deprive  someone—anyone—of  his
constitutional  right  to  be  free  from  racial  discrimination.  With
all  due  allowance  for  the  value  of  having  a  varied  group  of
students  to  hobnob  and  otherwise  interact  with  each  other
during  the  four  years  of medical  school,  or  three  years  of law
school,  we  are  most  surprised  to  see  it  seriously  urged  that
considerations  relating  to  the composition  of a  transient  student
body  outweigh,  on  a  constitutional  scale  of values,  the  rights
guaranteed  by the  Equal  Protection  Clause.

Since  the  proponents  of  reverse  discrimination  are  prob-
ably  incapable  of seeing  the  legitimacy  of this  argument  when
made  in  behalf  of  a  Bakke,  perhaps  they  might  grasp  the
preceding  point  more  easily  if the  situation  were  reversed,  and
an  almost  100%  black  state-run  college  began  to  practice  overt
discrimination  against  black  applicants  so  as  to  make  room  for
more  white  students,  thus  promoting  “diversity”  and  helping
both  blacks  and  whites.  Surely  that  would  be  unconstitutional
even  in Petitioners’  eyes.

Petitioners,  and  their  allies,  might  also  pause  to  reflect  that
the  very  benefits  to  be  obtained  from  racial  diversity  will
evaporate  if both  white  and  minority  students  realize  that  the
latter  were  admitted  preferentially  and  that  their  qualifications
are  well  below  those  of the  former.  It is precisely  this  kind  of

48

49

awareness—which  all  too  accurately  reflects  the  factual  situ-
ations  obtaining  today  in American  professional  schools,  gradu-
ate  schools,  and  colleges—that  leads  to  the  kinds  of attitudes
described  by a  young  black  attorney:

“Traditionally,  first-year  law  students  are  supposed  to  be
afraid,  or  at  least  awed;  but  our  fear  was  compounded  by
the  uncommunicated  realization  that  perhaps  we  were  not
authentic  law  students  and  the  uneasy  suspicion  that  our
classmates  knew  that  we  were  not,  and,  like  certain
members  of the  faculty,  had  developed  paternalistic  atti-
The  silence,  the  heavy  sense  of  ex-
tudes  toward  us.
pectation,  fell  on  all of the  blacks  in a classroom  whenever
one  of us  was  called  upon  for  an  answer.  We  waited,  with
the  class,  for the  chosen  man  to justify  the  right  of all of us
to  be there.  And  the  busy  silence  between  the  time  a  black
student  was  called  on  and  the  time  he  began  to  make  an
answer  was  alive  with  all our  answers  being  pushed,  by sad
attempts  at  some  kind  of empathy,  from  all  sections  of the
huge  room  to  the  mind  of the  man  who,  for  the  moment,
represented  all  of  us.
The  rest  of  the  class  would  wait
sometimes,  and  embarrassed  white  faces  would  turn  away
while  the  instructor  repeated  the  question,  and  papers
would  rattle  in signals  of sympathy.  And  when  an  answer
came,  however  poor  it was,  there  would  be  relief visible  in
the  faces  of  the  white  students  and  the  instructor,  and
audible  in  the  renewed  breathing  of the  rest  of the  black
students.  After  class,  the  student  who  had  been  called  on,
and  a  few  of his  friends,  might  walk  hurriedly  down  the
hall;  the  black  student  perhaps  apologizing,  perhaps
rationalizing  his  inability  to  give  a  better  answer.  At  such
times  it  would  not  be  unusual  for  a  white  friend  to  say,
“I’m  glad  he  didn’t  call  on  me.  I couldn’t  have  answered
that  question  myself.”  But  it would  be  very  unlikely  that
the  black  student  would  agree.”

J.A.  McPherson,  “The  Black  Law  Student:  A Problem

of Fidelities,”  Atlantic,  April  1970,  97,  99.

Another  writer  says  much  the  same:

“If in a given  class  the  great  majority  of the  black  students
are  at  the  bottom  of the  class,  this  factor  is bound  to  instill,
unconsciously  at  least,  some  sense  of intellectual  superior-
ity among  the  white  students  and  some  sense  of intellectual
inferiority  among  the  black  students.  Such  a Pairing  in the
same  school  of the  brightest  white  students  in the  country
with  black  students  of mediocre  academic  qualifications  is
social  experiment  with  loaded  dice  and  a  stacked  deck.
The  faculty  can  talk  around  the clock  about  disadvantaged
background,  and  it  can  excuse  inferior  performance  be-
cause of poverty,  environment,  inadequate  cultural  tradi-
tion,  lack  of educational  opportunity,  etc.  The  fact  remains
that  black  and  white  students  will  be exposed  to each  other
under  circumstances  in  which  demonstrated  intellectual
superiority  rests  with  the  whites.
If  to  compensate  for
disadvantaged
background,  the
faculty  discriminates
among  students  in  its  grading  and  marking,  its  double
standard  will  be  quickly  perceived  by  both  groups  of
students.  Because  of the  Law  School’s  current  admission
policy  the  difference  between  the  two  groups  will  be
centered  on  the  factor  of race.”

M.  Fleming,  “The  Black  Quota  at  Yale  Law  School”
[letter  to  Dean  Louis  Pollak],  The  Public  Interest  No.  19,
