H.  The  Argument  that  Minority  Professionals  Should  and
Will  “Return  to  the  Ghetto”  is Specious  and  Misleading
Petitioners,  and  some  of the  Amici  supporting  them,  such
as  the  Antioch  School  of  Law  and  Dr.  Jerome  A.  Lackner
(Director  of  California’s  Department  of  Health),  argue  that
minority  medical  graduates  are  more  likely  than  their  non-
minority  colleagues  to  practice  in  areas  of minority  concentra-
On  that  point,  the  findings  of  Dr.  James  L.  Curtis,
tion.
Associate  Dean  at Cornell  University  Medical  College,  are  most
germane.  Dr.  Curtis  is in charge  of minority  student  affairs  at
The  NAACP  Legal  Defense  and  Educational
his  school.
Fund’s  Brief Amicus,  at  65  n.189,  cites  Dr.  Curtis’  book  Blacks,
Medical  Schools,  and  Society  147  (1971)  as  authority  for  the
assertion  that  black  medical  students  tend  to  return  to  ghetto
neighborhoods  to  practice,  upon  graduation.  Petitioners’  Brief
also  cites  this  source.
Jd.,  25  n.24  at  26.  However,  what  Dr.
Curtis  really  says  is  that  black  students,  when  they  are  first
Dr.
applying  to  medical  school,  allege  that  they  will  do  so.
Curtis’  remarks  are  worth  quoting  at  length,  since  they  shed  a
different  light  on  this  question:

:

“Will  the  black  graduates  return  to  practice  medicine  in
the  ghetto?

>

.

.

I had  no  doubt  that  they  themselves  believe  it would  be  a
noble  and  altruistic  act  if they  were  to  open  up  an  office,
presumably  as  a  solo  practitioner,  and  become  a  general
practitioner  in  a  black  urban  ghetto.  Similar  impressions

were  found  from  my  conversations  with  other  medical
admissions  interviewers.  How  is this  explained?  Perhaps
most  superficially  it can  be explained  as  part  of the  current
black  student  ideology,  that  the student  feels  he is expected
at  least  to  make  such  a  statement.  It is easy  to  speculate
further  that  the  prospective  medical  student  probably
perceives  that  such  statements  seem  to  have  a  favorable
effect  on  other  black  students  who  might  otherwise  deride
his choice  of a conservative  field,  and  it might  also  have  a
favorable  effect  on  whites  who  harbor  either  romantic
liberal  or  segregationist  feelings.  My own  first  reaction  to
this  phenomenon  was  to  consider  it  a  current  fad  or
fashionable  view,  probably  of  a  passing  nature.
My
further  thoughts  were  that  some  of  them  undoubtedly
would  return  to  the  ghetto,  particularly  if they  had  for-
merly  lived  in  all-black  neighborhoods  as  almost  all  of
them  had,  but  that  they  would  remain  only  so  long  as  it
took  them  to  earn  enough  money  from  their  practice  to
move  away  to  a  suburb  or  to  a  more  desirable  neighbor-
hood  in the  city.

In  an  earlier  day,  P.B.  Cornely  pointed  out  that  Negro
physicians,  although  educated  at  Howard  and  Meharry
presumably  to  take  care  of the  health  needs  of the  great
mass  of southern  rural  Negroes,  were  actually  migrating  to
urban communities  to  serve  the  health  needs  of Negroes
migrating  to  more  prosperous  urban  communities.
In
precisely  the  same  way,  we  can  almost  certainly  predict
that  black  physicians  will  increasingly  migrate  to  the
suburbs  to  care  for  the  health  needs  of  the  more  pros-
perous  blacks  who  migrate  to  the  suburbs.”

Curtis,  Blacks,  Medical  Schools  and  Society  147-9  (1971)
( footnote  omitted  ).

|

$2

53

Dr.  Curtis  explains  that  these  students,  like  previous  black
physicians,  will  tend  to  work  mostly  with  black  patients,  id.  at
148-9,  but  he emphasizes:

“It  is  to  be  hoped  that  the  increased  numbers  of  black
physicians  who  will  be  receiving  superior  educational
preparations  for  their  successful  careers  as  physicians  will
be  utilized  as  physicians  for  the  whole  American  commu-
nity,  and  will  not be encouraged  to limit  their  work  to
black  patients  only.
With  increasing  desegregation  in
many  areas  of American  life,  and  an  increasing  receptivity
to  a racially  integrated  society,  it is most  unlikely  that  black
and  white  health-care  systems  and  markets  can  be  main-
tained,  even  if  racist  extremists  attempt  to  keep  these
segregated  systems  artificially  intact.”

Id.  at  162-3.

He  points  out  that  the  whole  question  of whether  minority
graduates  will  open  offices  in poor  minority  neighborhoods  may
be  irrelevant:

“It  is not  my  impression,  however,  that  the  great  bulk  of
medical  practice  and  health  care  in the  ghetto  is dispensed
from  doctors’  offices.  It is my  distinct  impression  that  most
black  patients  go  to  municipal  or  voluntary  hospitals  or
clinics  for  medical  care,  and  that  it is the  quality  of care
given  in  these  facilities  which  determines  their  health
outcome.
Certainly  this  is  true  for  the  more  serious  or
chronic  diseases  and  disorders,  those  requiring  the  most
complicated  diagnostic  procedures  and  therapeutic  prog-
rams.  Those  of my  black  professional  colleagues  who  are
the most  highly  trained  specialists  spend  a  large  part  of
their  time  in such  hospital  and clinic  settings,  and also
spend  part  of  their  time  in medical  school  teaching,
research,  or  administration.
Moreover,  most  of  my  col-
leagues  either  took part in, or had a lively interest  in, the
struggle  of black  professionals  to  gain  appointments  on  the
staff  of these  hospitals,  and  to  obtain  the  admission  and

equal  treatment  of  Negro  patients  to  these  public  and
private  community  institutions.  As  the  practice  of medi-
cine  has  become  increasingly  dependent  on  scientific  tech-
nology,  it has depended  more  and  more  on  the highly
The  solo
trained  specialist  staff  at  teaching  hospitals.
practitioner  practicing  in his office  in the ghetto,  or  in any
other  neighborhood,  is  only  one  small  element  in  the
medical  manpower  team.  It would  be  unfortunate  indeed
if a  disproportionately  large  share  of the  black  physicians
who  will  be graduating  during  the  next  decades  had  a too-
limited  view  of their  potential  medical  role.”

Id.  at  149-50.

Dr.  Curtis,  of  course,  is  saying  that  not  only  will  black
doctors  probably  nof  return  to  the  ghetto  and  remain  there,  but
that  this  fact  is  good  news.
Similar  sentiments  have  been
expressed  with  respect  to  minority  lawyers:

“It  is  a  proposition  laced  with  irony  that  cyclical  poverty
can be arrested  by taking  some  of the most  promising  and
talented  blacks,  placing  them  in  the  lowest-paying,  least-
powerful jobs in the legal  profession,  and  ‘sending’  them  to
the ghetto  where  they  will rarely  earn  enough  money  to
break  out  of the  ‘cycle’  themselves,  much  less  enhance  the
economic  st*tus  of their  clients.

If courts  are  to  insist  that  breaking  ‘cyclical  poverty’  must
be  the  /eitmotif  of  special  admissions,  perhaps  blacks
should  be  steered  towards  Wall  Street  where  they  can
obtain  a modicum  of wealth  and  prestige,  and  the ability  to
invest  in  black  enterprise.

Indeed,  argues  Norman  Redlich,  dean  of the  NYU  Law
School,  ‘It’s  important  for  black  lawyers  to  be  in  Wall
Street  firms;  white  clients  and  attorneys  should  see  black
lawyers.”  ”

Joyce  Curll,  NYU  Law  School’s  admissions  director,
agrees:  ‘If we  stereotype  minorities  simply  as  working  in

54

55

community  law  offices,  then  another  leap will  be necessary
20 years  hence  to  get  them  into  corporate  law  firms.  They
have  a  right  to  be  there  now.’

Lloyd  Zimmerman,  “Minority  Admissions  and  the
Courts,”  Legal  Focus  (Published  by  the  Second  Circuit,
American  Bar  Association/Law  Student  Division)  1,  2
(October,  1976).

Thus,  the  “return  to  the  ghetto”  argument  is  not  one  on

which  Petitioners  can  rely.

I.

Reverse  Discrimination  Is  Decidedly  Not.A  Means  Of
Improving  Minorities’  Health  Status

While  it  cannot  be  denied  that  programs  of  reverse
discrimination  are  tempting  indeed  to  lazy  professional  school
administrators  who  are  looking  for  some  way  to  impart  the
notion  that  they  have  a  commitment  to  helping  minority
groups,  the  fact  remains  that  this  Court  has  “never  held
administrative  convenience  to  justify  racial  discrimination.”
Douglas,  J.,  Dissenting,  De  Funis  v.  Odegaard,  416  U.S.  312,
341  (1974).

There  are  ways  available  to  meet  the  health  needs  of the
minority  community  without  resorting  to  preferential  treatment
(which  would  actually  Aurt  the  minority  community ).

After  pointing  out,  correctly,  that  minority  groups  have
desperate  needs  for  better  health  care,  Petitioners  then  make  a
quantum  jump  and  allege  that  producing  more  minority  doctors
is  necessary  to  solve  such  problems.  That  assertion  is  totally
wrong.  (Let  it be  noted,  of course,  that  even  if the  statement
were  true  it would  not  excuse  the  use  of racial  discrimination  to
produce  minority  doctors.  )'7

17  This is  so  because,  under  the Constitution,  officia!  racial  discrimination
is  simply  foreclosed  as a  route  to  alleged  social  progress.  Any  government
to think  up  new answers  to our country’s problems  must  look
leader  wishing
elsewhere,  for state  discrimination is  not  a permissible  option in the  American
ations,  and  much
system.

It  has  taken  over  a  hundred  years,  countless

one

oe

But  the  fact  remains  that  it is not  true.  And  to demonstrate
its  falsity,  we  must  expose  the  two-faced  nature  of the  propo-
nents  of  reverse  discrimination  in  medical  school  admissions.
In  forums  such  as  this  Court,  they  declaim  about  a  supposed
desperate  need  for  physicians  from  minority  groups,  who,  they
insist,
in-
be  “produced”  only  through
stitutionalized  racial  discrimination.  But  when  the  topic  shifts
from  defending  reverse  discrimination  to  improving  health  care
these  same  medical  authorities  along  with  their  colleagues,
express  markedly  inconsistent  views.

rampant

can

They  are  generally  agreed  that  merely  producing  more
new  physicians  ( without  regard  to  their  race)  will  not  have  an
appreciable  impact  on  the  nation’s  health  in  general  or  the
health  of our  poorer  citizens  in particular.  Other  measures,  such
as  the  judicious  use  of  auxiliary  medical  personnel  in  new,
innovative  ways,  and  a vastly  expanded  emphasis  on  preventive
medicine,  are  what  will  really  work.  While  this  statement  may
well  come  as  a  surprise  to  those  unfamiliar  with  the  contempo-
rary  professional  literature  on  health  care  delivery,  this  is what
the  experts  in  the  field  believe.

negligible

clinics,  health  care  workers,  or  removing  hospital  beds,  has

from  preventive  measures  and  better  patient  education,  not

sor  of medicine.  Eisenberg,  “The  Search  for Care,”  235,  244-5,
in J.H.  Knowles  (ed.),  Doing Better  and Feeling  Worse:  Health
in the  United  States
(1977)  (also  published  as  the  Winter  1977
issue  of Daedalus).  And  the  Vice  President  for Health  Sciences
at  the  University  of Arizona,  Tucson,  Dr.  M.K.  Du Val,  tells  us:
“Most  of the  factors  that  bear  on  our  health  as  individuals  lie
outside  the  control  of  traditional  medicine.”  DuVal,  “The
Provider,  the  Government,  and  the  Consumer,”  185,  192,  in
J.H.  Knowles  (ed.),  id.

He  explains  that  health,  including  not  mere  freedom  from
diagnosable  disease,  but  the  functioning  of the  body  and  mind
at  their  full  capacities,  “[i]s  only  marginally  affected  by
medical  care;  indeed,  much  of  contemporary  scientific  medi-
cine,”  with  its  focus  on  disease,  “  is almost  irrelevant  to  good
health.”  Instead,  “[t]}he  individual’s  behavior,  his  environ-
ment,  and  his  living  habits  have  a  far  greater  impact  on  his
health  than  anything  medicine  might  do  for  him.”  /d@.,  185.  In
his  own  essay,  the  book’s  editor,  Dr.  John  H.  Knowles,
documents  this  thesis  in  exhaustive  detail,  with  separate  dis-
cussions  of the  most  prevalent  and  serious  diseases.  Knowles,
“The  Responsibility  of the  Individual,”  id.,  57.

Hopefully,  the  passage  of the  National  Consumer  Health
Information  and  Health  Promotion  Act  of  1976,  P.L.  94-317
(Title  I),  which  is  directed  to  meet  these  specific  needs,  will
help  move  the  nation  forward  in this  area.

With  respect  to  innovations  in  the  use  of auxiliary  health
manpower,  see  Committee  for  Economic  Development,  Build-
ing a  National  Health  Care  System  80-3  (1973);  U.S.  Dept.  of
Health,  Education,  and  Welfare,  Trends  Affecting  the  U.S.
Health  Care  System,  (Dept.  of H.E.W.  Publication  No.  HRA
76-14503)  373-81  (1976).

me

a ee

Eli  Ginzberg,  Professor  of Economics  at  Columbia  and  a
nationally  known  expert  on  manpower  problems,  cautions  that
“(T]he  direction  of physicians  into  the  ghetto  may  make  little
sense  if they  are  to  provide  routine  ambulatory  care.
Nurse
practitioners  and  health  aides  drawn  from  the  community  are
likely  to  do  a  better  job,  especially  if they  are  effectively  linked
to  hospitals.”  Ginzberg,  “Health  Services,  Power  Centers,  and
Decision-Making  Mechanisms,”  203,  207,  in  J.H.  Knowles
(ed.),  Doing  Better  and  Feeling  Worse:  Health  in  the  United
States  (1977)  (also  published  as  the  Winter  1977  issue  of
Daedalus).
Furthermore,  such  allied  health  personnel  are
much  more  likely  to  spend  time  on  patient  education  and
working  with  patients  on  preventive  measures  than  are  physi-
cians.
After  all,  for  many  years  white  doctors  have  been
repeatedly  accused  of not  spending  nearly  enough  time  on  these
subjects  with  their  white  middle  class  patients.
It  is  unlikely
that  a  physician’s  race  would  make  much  different  in  this
attitude.  Indeed,  it would  be  unwise  for  a  doctor  to  emphasize
such  efforts—vital  as  they  are—inasmuch  as  his  training  in
other  areas,  to  fulfill  duties  which  only  he  can  perform,  would
be  wasted.
Allied  personnel  and  paraprofessionals  should
handle  these  tasks,  which  if  well  done,  will  prevent  the
deteriorations  and  complications  that  a  physician  alone  can
cope  with.

In one  experiment,  paraprofessionals  with  only  four  weeks
of training  were  used  successfully  to  monitor  long-term  ambula-
tory  diabetic  and  hypertensive  patients,  most  of  them  black.
Through  use  of  computer-derived  “clinical  algorithms,”  also
called  “problem-oriented  protocols,”  the  paraprofessionals
were  able  to determine  which  clinic  patients  needed  to see  a
doctor  and  which  could  be sent  home  safely.  Only in  1% of the
instances  was  a symptom  missed  ( and  that  was  judged  to  be an
unimportant  symptom).
This  is  only  one  example  of  how
“physician  extenders”  can  function  to  relieve  the  doctor’s  load
and  render  better  service.  The  patients  themselves  were  very
satisfied,  and  especially  appreciated  the  chance  to  obtain

58

59

psychosocial  support  from  the paraprofessionals,
with  whom
they felt free to discuss  matters  about  which  they felt reluctant
to consume
et al., “Protocols
the doctors’  time.  A.L.  Komaroff,
for  Physician  Assistants:  Management  of  Diabetes  and
Hypertension,”  290  New  Engl.  J.Med.  307  (1974).

J.  Even  Now,  Reverse  Discrimination
Is Polarizing  And

Balkanizing  American  Society

Looking  at  the Briefs  Amicus  filed in this case provides  a
disconcerting  glimpse  at  the  possible  future  of race  relations  in
America.

First,  it  is ominous  that  over  three  dozen  Briefs  Amicus
were  filed  on  behalf of Petitioners,  representing  many  dozens  of
otherwise  respectable  and  progressive  organizations,  all  saying
that  a  person’s  entire  career  and  chances  for  happiness  can  be
consigned  to the waste  heap  because  of  his race—and  this in
the  interest  of social  justice!  This  is a  measure  of  how  crooked
many  people’s  thinking  has  become  on  this  issue.

Secondly,  the  Amici  on  behalf  of  Respondent  (although
not all of  them  are known  to counsel  at  the time  the instant
brief  is  being  written)—appear  to  include  representatives  of
several  ethnic  groups,  such  as  Polish-Americans  and  Italian-
Americans,  and  several  organizations  representing  America’s
Jewish  population.  Also, there is at  least one individual  Amicus
on behalf  of  Bakke  who  is himself  a  prospective  victim  of
*  reverse  discrimination,  since he is now applying  to law school:

J. Hoy.
Timothy

Third,  the  fact  that  so  many  otherwise  allied  organizations
are now  filing  briefs  on opposite  sides  of this case  is but  a
precursor  of the polarization  and hate  that  will  develop  if the
below  is reversed.
decision

Reversal  would indeed  lead to the creation—in  a different
sense  from  that originally  described—of  the “two  Americas”
postulated  by the report  of the National  Advisory  Commission
on Civil Disorders (the Kerner  Commission).  That  document

white—separate  and  unequal.”  Report  of the  National  Adviso-
ry  Commission  on  Civil  Disorders  |  (Bantam  edition,  1968).
In  the praiseworthy  effort  to reverse  that  trend,  we are now
creating  two different  sucieties  in another  sense:  a collection  of
rather  arbitrarily  chosen  minority  groups  whose  members  are
automatically  given official  and  unofficial  preferences  in various
respects,  and  the  rest  of  us.  N.  Glazer,  Affirmative  Dis-
crimination  197  (1975).

Exacerbating  matters  is the  fact  that  the  choices  of benefi-
ciary  groups  to  receive  affirmative  action  preferences  are  arbi-
trary  and  capricious.  Jd.  198-99.

It is also  been  pointed  out  that  98%  of Hispanics  are  white,
which  raises  the  question  of why  they  should  be  favored  over
other  whites.  Brief Amicus  of Polish  American  Congress,  et.  al.,
12.

Still  another  critic  has  spoken  to  the  same  point:

“Puerto  Ricans,  Spaniards,  Argentinians,  and  Mexicans
are  all ‘Spanish  surnamed.’  It is hard  to  see  what  else  they
have  in  common,  or  why  they  should  receive  different
treatment  than  Portuguese,  Brazilians,  or  Arabs.  And  it is
not  obvious  that  various  white  ‘ethnics’  suffered  less  from
discrimination  than  females  did.  Yet  they  are  not  prefer-
entially  admitted,  or  hired,  by  affirmative  action.  These
remedies,  then,  are  not  only  counterproductive  and  unjust
most  of the time,  but also arbitrarily  selective.  Perhaps  this
is a  minor  defect  compared  to those  already  mentioned:  if

“There  is every  reason  to  believe—indeed,  we  already  see

women  in  the  past.

Could  Backfire,”  Psychology  Today,  August  1977,  18.

The  magnitude  of the  decision  in the  instant  case  will  carry
far  beyond  the  specific  question  of professional  school  admis-
sions  at  state  universities  tomorrow  or  even  state  university
admissions  in  general.
Indeed,  judging  from  the  comment
appearing  in the  mass  media,  it would  be taken  as  a  bellwether
decision  that  will  indicate  whether  reverse  discrimination  in
diverse  sectors  of American  life  is permissable.  Admissions  to
privately  run  universities,  and  employment  (especially  public
employment,  where  equal  protection  guarantees  apply ), will  be
dramatically  affected.

members.
Many  governmental  entities  and  private  corpo-

Again  we  see  the  arbitrary  selection  of  preferred  minor-
ities,  with,  again,  one  minority  group  getting  even  more  prefer-
ence  than  the  other  preferred  groups.
Such  a  preferential
scheme  was  invalidated  by Flanagan  v.  President  and Directors
of  Georgetown  College,  417  F.Supp.  377  (D.D.C.  1976).
Although  the  institution  covered  was  a  private  university’s  law
school  (the  Georgetown  Law  Center),  the  institution  had
received  federal  financial  aid  and  was  thus  covered  by Title  VI
of the  Civil  Rights  Act  of  1964,  42  USC  § 2000d  et  seg.  The
federal  monies  were  received  for  the  purpose  of constructing
the  Law  Center’s  building—and  this  was  found  to  be  sufficient
to  bring  the  school’s  practices  under  the  ambit  of Title  VI.  417
F.Supp.  at  383-4.  The  suit  arose  because  financial  aid  funds
were  awarded  on  a  reverse-discriminatory  basis  by the  school.
These  were  its  own,  not  federal  funds,  but  once  Title  VI
coverage  was  found,  that  statute  barred  the school’s  preferential
policies.
The  court  in  Flanagan  expressly  held  that  the
defendants’  position  is “reverse  discrimination  on  the  basis  of
race  which  cannot  be justified  by a  claim  of affirmative  action.”
417  F.Supp.  384.  It went  on  to  explain  that:

“While  an  affirmative  action  may  be appropriate  to  ensure
that  all  persons  are  afforded  the  same  opportunities  or  are
considered  for  benefits  on  the  same  basis,  it  is  not  per-
missible  when  it  allocates  a  scarce  resource  (be  it jobs,
housing,  or  financial  aid)  in  favor  of  one  race  to  the
detriment  of others.”  Jd.,  384.

We  submit  that  the  decision  in  the  instant  case—which
ever  way  it  goes—will  have  great  effects  on  the  behavior  of
Americans  in everyday  transactions  and  decisions,  not  because
of  the  narrow  holding  therein,  but  because,  in  the  popular
mind,  the  instant  case  is viewed  as  dealing  with  the  legality  of
preferential  treatment  in  any  selective  process,  wherein  only
some  persons  can  be  chosen  and  others  must  be  rejected.  We
believe  that,  for  a  large  number  of  Americans  indeed,  the
decision  herein  will  be  the  decision  on  the  legality  of  such
preferential  policies  and  practices.

64

65

Actually,  in  a  sense,  this  could  be  said  to  be  true,  for  the
instant  case  presents  a  fundamental  and  far  reaching  challenge
to  the American  tradition.  We  greatly  fear  that  once  the  door  is
opened  to  allow  “affirmative  discrimination,”  there  will  be  no
stopping  this  juggernaut.  That  is why  we  must  adhere  to  the
wise  advice  the  inventor  of that  apt  phrase  has  given  us:

This,  I believe,  is what  was  intended  by the  Constitution
and  the  Civil  Rights  Act,  and  what  most  of the  American
people—in  all the various  ethnic  and racial  groups  that
make  it up—believe  to  be the  measure  of a good  society.  It
is now our task  to work  with the intellectual,  judicial,  and
political  institutions  of  the  country  to  reestablish  this
simple  and  clear  understanding,  that  nghts  attach  to  the

exercised  without  distinction  of  race,  color,  or  national

m

Race  Is  Not  A  “Bona  Fide  Occupational  Qualification”
For  Professionals

Numerous  briefs  filed in this  case  assert  that  minority

.
These  arguments  are  based  in  the  notion  that  group
identification  can  be  a  proper  criterion  for  employment.  Be-
cause in certain  cases this can be true, the Civil Rights Act of
1964,  as  amended,  provides  that  religion,  national  origin or  sex,
but  not  race,  can  be  a  “bona  fide  occupational  qualification,”
usually  abbreviated  as  “BFOQ.”  § 703(e)(1);  42  U.S.C.
§ 2000e-2(e)(1).
However,  the  BFOQ  concept  has—quite
rightly—been  interpreted  narrowly  by the  Equal  Employment
Opportunity  Commission  and  the courts,  and, in general,  covers
only such  workers  as  locker  room  attendants  (who  should  be of
the same  sex as those  who use that facility  to protect  users’
privacy  and  modesty),  actors  or  actresses  (who  need  to  be
chosen on the  basis  of  sex  for  purposes  of  authenticity  in
performance)  or  editors  of denominational  newspapers  (who
must  belong  to  the  particular  faith  in order  to  function  properly
in such  a  position ).

Professional  practice,  however,  is  not  such  a  situation.
Skills,  not  group  membership,  are  the  relevant  factors.  Nor  is
there  a  mght  possessed  by patients,  or  law  clients,  to  be  served
by their  racial  or  ethnic  confreres.

-

-- ee ee

Even  if it  were  assumed  that  producing  more  minority
professionals  would  provide  a greater  number  of “role  models”
for minority  youth,  the individual  right  of  each  applicant  for
admission  to  a  state-run  medical  or  law  school  (or for employ-
ment  as  a physician  or  attorney)  not  to  be discriminated  against
is guaranteed  by law,  whereas  the wish  to have  numerous  “role
models”  in young  people’s  environments  is only a desideratum.
Indeed,  while  increasing  the  number  of  physicians  or
attorneys  from  minority  groups  may  be psychologically  benefi-
cial for the latter,  it can produce  a  negative  impact  on those
very minorities,  the medical  profession  and the bar, patients
and clients  of all races,  and the community  as a  whole  if these
doctors  and  lawyers  are  known  to have  been  admitted  to
medical  or law schoci,  and/or  hired  on the  basis  of race  or
ethnicity—because  of  the  stigmatizing  effect  of  reverse  dis-
crimination.

68

69

The  argument  is made  that  preferential  treatment  is justi-
fied  because  minority  group  doctors  would  be  more  attuned  to
the  subcultures  and  folkways  of their  brethren  and  thus  better
able  to  aid  them.  It would  seem  that  if this  argument  had  any
validity  at  all,  it would  hold  true  especially  for  one  particular
specialty,  psychiatry,  because  of the  tremendous  importance  of
communication  between  doctor  and  patient,  and  the  critical
need  for  the  doctor  to  understand  the  patient’s  cultural
background.  But  the  facts  are  otherwise.

To  quote  a  black  psychiatrist,  expressing  his  strong  dis-
approval  of  a  1969  student  rebellion  at  Howard  University’s
College  of  Medicine,  when  separatist-minded  black  students
sought  to  increase  the  school’s  “blackness,”

“...  there  were  assertions  that  a  white  psychiatrist  on  the
faculty  could  not  teach  black  students  anything  about  a
psychiatric  disorder  such  as  schizophrenia.
The  basic
metabolic  disturbances  which  apparently  underlie  the
schizophrenic  disease  process,  and  the  psychopathologic
disturbances  which  are  known  to occur  in schizophrenics  of
all  ethnic  and  social  class  groups,  are  given  modified
expressions  in  various  cultural  or  subcultural  settings,  but
there  is not  the  slimmest  evidence  that  diagnosis  or  treat-
ment  or  rehabilitation  can  be  based  on  a  patient’s  skin

Dr.  Curtis’  colleagues  agree  with  him,  even  with  respect  to
a  psychiatric  problem  that  should  present a still  stronger  case
for  the  viewpoint  that  minority  patients  need  minority  doctors:
serving  the  Spanish-speaking  patient  of  low  socioeconomic
Status:

“although  many  have  been  in  the  United  States  for
substantial  periods  of time,  they  often  have  not  learned  to
speak  English.  ...One  study  of patients  who  showed  an
unwillingness  to  learn  English  revealed  that  many  have

guage.  They  were  mistrustful  not  only of English-speaking
doctors,  but  of Spanish-speaking  doctors  as  well.”

R.  A.  MacKinnon  and  R.  Michels,  The  Psychiatric

Interview  in  Clinical  Practice  394  (1971).

The  same  textbook,  in its chapter  on  interviewing  non-English
speaking  patients  through  an  interpreter,  advises  that  “under
most  circumstances,  the  patient  will  develop  a  transference  [in
the  technical  psychoanalytic  sense]  to  the  physician  just  as  if
the  interpreter  were  not  present.”  Jd.  at  454.

These  writers  are  well  aware  of  problems  caused  by the
medical  folkways  of minority  group  persons.  They  show  how
the solution  to these  difficulties  is not to provide  a  doctor  from
the  same  race  or  ethnic  group,  but  simply  to  handle  the
Situation  in  a  professional  manner:

“[ I]t is common  for  the  lower  class  Puerto  Rican,  Cuban,
or  Mexican  patient  to  feel  that  his  trouble  has  been  caused

To  further  demonstrate  the fallaciousness  of the nouon
that  “You've  got to be one in order  to help  one,”  we can
examine  a  striking  example,  from California,  involving  the legal
profession.  In a  murder  case  an appellate  court  has held  that a
judge  assigning  counsel  to represent  an indigent  defendant
should  not  take  the attorney’s  race  into  consideration.  The
defendant,  a  black  man,  had been  convicted  by a  jury  of first
degree  murder.  Sentenced  to death,  he appealed,  raising
among  other  grounds  of alleged  error the trial court’s  demai  of
his motion  for a  change  of counsel  and for a  continuance.  He
contended  that  race  was  a  relevant  criterion  for choosing
counsel,  and that  having  a  white  lawyer  had prejudiced  his
nghts.  In unanimously  commuting  his sentence  to life impris-

that:

“Any  limitation  of Court  appointments  based  on race  or
ethnic  background  has no place  in the administration  of
justice.  The  right  to be free  from  discrimination
is an
individual  nght, and it should  not be abndged  or modified
on the basis of an individual's  race  or other  ethnic  factors.
The  deliberate  preference  for an individual  of one race,
and  solely  because  of his race,  over  an individual  of
another  race,  even  in cases  where  the latter  individual  is
better  qualified  in all other respects,  is a grave injustice  and
an unconscionable
violation  of constitutional  principles  of
equal  protection.”

People  v.  Fitzgerald,  29 Cal.  App. 3d 296, 309-10,  105

Cal.  Rptr.  458,  466  (1972).

In  this  example,  of course,  the “right  to be  free  from
discrimination”  under  discussion  is that  of applicants  for the
post  of assigned  counsel,  not any right  of the  defendant.
However,  this  decision  does  not  impair  the rights  of the
defendant—it  enhances  them.  His mght is to receive  competent
counsel.  The  court  correctly  ruled  that  race is irrelevant  in
a good  lawyer.
choosing

The  defendant  may  have  thought  that  only  an attorney  of
his  own  race  could  communicate  well  enough  with  him  to
prepare  the most  effective  defense  possible,  and to inspire  his
confidence  enough  to elicit  the necessary  cooperation.  How-
ever,  to reiterate,  just  as some  members  of one  race  are
especially  gifted  in communicating  with  members  of another,
there  are many  people—both  blacks  and  whites—who  have
considerable  difficulty  relating  to and  communicating  with
members  of their  own race,  especially  across  cultural  or socio-
economic  lines.

Clearly,  in choosing  the best  qualified  candidate  for the
assigned  counsel  position,  a  court  will  want  someone  skilled
and  experienced  in  the practice  of criminal  law.  An obvious
ingredient  for success  in such practice  is the ability  to engage  in
a  close working  relationship  with one’s  clients  who—in  the case
of a  criminal  lawyer—are  often  people  whose  background  is
very different  from  that of counsel,  even if they are of the same
race.  Therefore,  the legitimate  need  of a  defendant  to obtain
an  attorney  who  can  relate  to  him  and  whom  he  can
trust—which  the defendant  may  mistakenly  think  would  be
protected  by making  race  a criterion  in lawyer  selection—will
actually  be  safeguarded  automatically  if the  best-qualified
candidate  critenon  is employed,  as required  by the Fitzgerald
decision.

Furthermore,  Petitioners  and the Amici  supporting  them
fail to realize  that  the  BFOQ  argument  can  be used—if  it is
a double-edged  sword.  Would  it not be equally
accepted—as
true  that  a  white  doctor  or lawyer  is better  qualified  than  a

74

75

black  one  to  handle  cases  involving  white  patients  or  clients?
This  view,  carried  to  its logical  conclusion,  is really  an  argument
for  racially  segregated  medical  and  legal  professions.

In  the  field  of  education,  similar  arguments  have  been
made  in  favor  of  imposing  a  racial  BFOQ  in  selection  of
teachers  and  professors  ( despite  the  Federal  statutory  exclusion
of  race  as a  BFOQ  category).  Such  a line  of thought would
lead to a  restoration  of the segregated  educational  system  struck
down  by the  Supreme  Court  over  two decades  ago,  in Brown  v.
Board  of Education  of  Topeka,  347  U.S.  483  (1954)  and  a
return  to  the  discredited  “separate  but  equal”  doctrine  of Plessy
v.  Ferguson,  163  U.S.  537  (1896).  The  Equal  Employment
Opportunity  Commission,  recognizing  that  fact,  has  soundly
E.E.O.C.  Decision  No.
repudiated  this  misguided  argument.
74-106,  10  FEP  Cases  269,  CCH.  EEOC.  Dec.  § 6427
(1974)  at  n.20.  The  E.E.0.C.,  in  holding  illegal  an  instance  of
reverse  discrimination  against  a  white  professor,  approvingly
cited  Smith  v.  Board  of Education,  365  F.2d  770  (8th  Cir.
1966),  where  the court flatly  rejected  the argument  of  an all-
white  school  board  that  black  teachers  should  not be hired  to
instruct  white  children  because  of a  lack  of “rapport.”  Decision
No.  74-106,  supra,  at  n.16  and  accompanying  text.  And  the
New  York  Court  of Appeals,  interpreting  the equal  protection
provision of that State’s  Constitution  in a  case involving alleged
preferential  treatment  for minorities  in appointing  school  super-
visors  in a district  with  a  heavily  minority  enrollment,  held that
“race  or religion  cannot,  as qualifying  factors,  constitutionally
be considered...  .” Council
Education,  23  N.Y.  2d  458,  468,  245  N.E.  2d  204,  210  ( 1969).

L.  Professional  Schools’  Minority  Enrollment  “Goals”  Are

Really  Quotas

Petitioners’  Brief  goes  to  incredible  lengths  to  deny  the
obvious  when  it alleges  ( at 46)  that  there  is no  “quota”  against
whites  in  the  instant  case.  That  statement  is absurd.  A  fixed
number  of places—usually  16—are  set  aside  each  year  for  the
exclusive  use  of racial  minorities.  A  minority  applicant  to  the
medical  school  seeks  one  of  100  available  places,  including
approximately  16 a  year  reserved  solely  for  minorities.  A non-
minority  applicant,  such  as  Bakke,  only  has  the  residue—84
(Furthermore,  he  may  well  face
places—available  to  him.
reverse  discrimination  even  in  the  competition  for  those  84
“regular”  slots,  also,  despite  any  denials  by  the  medical
school.)  Thus,  16  percent  fewer  places  are  open  to  a  non-
minority  applicant  than  are  open  to  a  minority  applicant.
Surely  this  flagrant  denial  of equal  opportunity  richly  deserves
the  appellation  of “quota  system.”

Petitioners’  Brief,  at  45-47,  and  the  American  Bar  Associ-
ation  Brief Amicus,  at  20,  both  try to draw the same  fraudulent
dichotomy  between  quotas  (which  they  admit  are  impermis-
sible),  and  “goals”  (which  they  claim  are  permissible).  They
allege  that it was  “goals”  that  were  resorted  to by the medical
school  in the instant case.  Petitioners’  discussion  of this  point
does not attempt  to define  “goals.”  But the simple  facts are that
the medical  school  discriminates  against  white  applicants  and  in
favor  of minority  applicants.  No amount  of circumlocutions or
verbal  gymnastics  by the medical  school  can obscure  or deny
that  ugly  fact—or  can  in any  way  succeed  in denying  the
suffering,  frustration  and utter  helplessness  of  the victims  who
are now told  that their years of diligent  effort  and sacrifice,  and
their  hopes  and ambitions  are all to be simply  disregarded  and
turned  aside,  so that  the University  can point to.a high  minority
“body  count”  as evidence  of its devotion  to social  justice.

These  goals  are not mere  innocent  targets;  they  are num-
bers the attainment  of which  is sought  as  an end in itself.  Since

76

77

.

a quota  is “a  share  .
.  assigned  to  a group,”  American  Heritage
Dictionary  of the  English  Language  1073  (1970),  Petitioners’
“goals”  fit  the  definition  of quota  precisely,  whether  or  not  a
rigid  number  is set  before  the  admissions  process  begins  each
year.  Cf.  Petitioners’  Brief,  46-7.

We  note  that  the  Secretary  of  Health,  Education,  and
Welfare  has  also,  in  recent  public  statements,  begun  to  draw
what  we  consider  to  be  this  distinction  without  a  difference
between  goals  and  quotas.  N.Y.  Times,  June  6, 1977,  1; June  9,
1977,  Al7.

Perhaps  the  most  revealing  light  on  the  nature  of  these
“goals”  appears  in  a memorandum  dated  August  11,  1972  from
Martin  S.  Begun,  Associate  Dean  at  New  York  University
School  of Medicine,  to  Dr.  Davis  G.  Johnson  of the  Division  of
Student  Affairs,  Association  of  American  Medical  Colleges.
Entitled  “Legal  Considerations  Related  to  Minority  Group
Recruitment  and  Admissions,”  it was  apparently  distributed  at
a  June  22,  1972  meeting  of the  Association’s  Northeast  Regio-
nal  Group  on  Student  Affairs,  held  at  Burlington,  Vermont.

The  memorandum,  which  favors  preferential  admissions,

candidly  states  as  follows:

“Establishing  given  percentages  or  quotas  of  minority
students  to  be  accepted  in  a  class  represents  predictable
problems.
It  is
possible  to  achieve  the  same  results  without  giving  the
appearance  of  restricting  portions  of  the  class  for  desig-
nated  groups.”

This  should  be  avoided  at  all  costs.

Id.  at  9.

Precisely.  It is just  such  attempts  at  duplicity  that  underlie  the
efforts  of Petitioners,  the  Amici  in  support  of them  who  adopt
this  concept,  and  the  Secretary  of  HEW  to  rely  on  the  false
dichotomy  between  goals  and  quotas.

Significantly,  when  this  memorandum  was  published  in the
Journal  of Medical  Education,  the  third  sentence  was  deleted.
Begun,  “Legal  Considerations  Related  to  Minority  Group
Recruitment  and  Admissions,”  48  J.  Med.  Educ.  556,  559
(1973).

Law  schools  use  the  same  ploy.  According  to  an  article  on

minority  admissions  in  the  law  schools  in  New  York  City:

“While  the  representation  of  minority  students  at  the
schools  interviewed  for  this  article  varies  widely  from  2
percent  at  Brooklyn  to  12  percent  at  Hofstra,  the  deans
unanimously  denied  the  use  of  any  quotas  for  minority
acceptance.

In  fact,  more  of  the  deans  admitted  having  a  target
number  in  mind  for  a  desired  proportion  of the  entering
class.”

OR RU Ur ee ee Re Ro

R.  Hochberger,  “Law  Deans  Divided  on  Issue  of
Special  Minority  Admissions,”  N.Y.L.J.,  Feb.  25,  1977,
is

The  process  is the  same  at  the  Harvard  Law  School:

“No  one  talks  about  a  precise  quota,  but  all  the  members
of the  [admissions]  committee  know  that  minorities  have
constituted  12  to  14  percent  of past  classes.  Conducting
their  discussion  in  terms  of how  far  down  the  list  to  go,
they  accept  enough  minority  applicants  to  reach  the  com-
monly  understood  target.”

J.W.  Foster,  “Race  and  Truth  at  Harvard,”  The  New

Republic,  July  17,  1976,  16,  20.

Thus,  it should  be quite  clear  that,  whatever  one’s  opinion
of quota  systems,  it is undeniably  evident  that  Petitioners,  and
“most  professional  schools  in  this  country  today  utilize”  these
devices.  Petitioners’  Brief,  14.

78

79

M.  Reverse  Discrimination  Requires  Intrusive  Governmental

Assigning  Of  Racial  and  Ethnic  Labels

One  further—and  especially  ugly—aspect  of  reverse  dis-
crimination  is that,  as  the  great  value  of being  a member  of an
officially  preferred  minority  group  becomes  manifest,  various
factual  questions  will  arise  as  to  whether  a particular  person  can
or  cannot  justly  claim  such  a  status.
In  what  may  be  only  a
precursor  of  what  is  to  come,  an  arbitrator  has  ruled  that  a
Nevada  engineering  company,  which  had  announced  a  pro-
gram  of  preferential  treatment  in  employment  for  American
Indians,  could  force  an  alleged  Indian  claiming a right  to such  a
preference  to  document  his  ancestry.  Wall  Street  Journal,  June
29,  1975,  at  1, col.  5.  The  frightening  similarity  to  Hitler’s
Nuremberg  Laws  is obvious.  These  Amici  believe  that  such  a
trend,  with  all  its  deeply  disturbing  implications,  must  be
stopped,  and  stopped  quickly.

Perhaps  the  best  clue  as  to  where  Petitioners  themselves
are  headed  in this  area  of ethnic  categorization  would  be  their
proposed  Ethnic  Identity  Transmittal  Form  U1605  for  new
employees,  which  we  reprint,  post,  as  an  Appendix.  The  June
13,  1977  covering  letter  from  University  Vice  President
Kleingartner  states  that  the  new  form  was  approved  by  the
University’s  Office  of General  Counsel,  which  also  had  a  major
responsibility  for  preparing  Petitioners’  Brief,  thus  providing  a
chilling  preview  of what  is in  store,  no  doubt,  for  all  persons
connected  in any  way  with  Petitioners’  University.

At  present,  in  medical  school  admissions,  the  University
relies  on  a form  used  nationally  for applying  to  medical  schools.
Therefore,  we  might  profitably  take  a  closer  look  at  this
American  Medical  College  Application  Service  form,  described
in Petitioners’  Brief,  40 n.51  at  41.  The  version  for the  1978-79
entering  class  asks  “what  is your  racial/ethnic  self description?”
Spaces  are  provided  for the  following  responses:  black,  Ameri-
can  Indian  or  Alaskan  native,  white,  Asian  or  Pacific  Islander,
Hispanic  ( with  subdivisions  for  Mexican/ American  or  Chicano,
Puerto  Rican  [mainland],  Puerto  Rican  [Commonwealth],

Cuban,  and  other  Hispanic.)  The  next  question  says:  “If  you
wish  to  be  considered  as  a  minority  group  agplicant  designate
reason:  Ethnic  [or]  financial  [or]  ethnic  and  financial.”  These
questions  are  not  optional;  they  are  to  be  answered  by  all
applicants.

Nor  is this  a  mere  isolated  instance.  The  registration  form
used  by  the  Law  School  Data  Assembly  Service  includes  an
optional  “ethnic  question,”  to  facilitate  the  use  of  race  and
ethnicity  in the  admissions  process  by law  schools  participating
in  the  Law  School  Candidate  Referral  Service.
Law  School
Admission  Council,  Law  School  Admission  Bulletin  1976-77,  at
9-10  (1976).

It is also  relevant  to  consider  the  ethnic  question  used  on
the  College  Entrance  Examination  Board's  registration  forms
for  the  Scholastic  Aptitude  Test  and  other  examinations  given
by that  body  for  such  applicants.  The  question  “How  do  you
describe  yourself?”  on  the  1976-77  form  has  seven  choices:
“American  Indian,  Black  or  Afro-American  or  Negro,  Mexican-
American  or  Chicano,  Oriental  or  Asian-American,  Puerto
Rican,  White  or  Caucasian,  Other.”  It  is  prefaced  by  the
following  rather  disingenuous  statement:

“The  College  Board  wants  its  tests  and  services  to  be  fair
and  useful  to  all  candidates.  Research  based  on  responses
to  questions  37  and  38  will  help  the  College  Board
evaluate  and  improve  its  tests  and  services.
Your  re-
sponses  will  also  be  reported  to  your  school  and  to  those
colleges  that  accept  such  information  in order  to  make  sure
their  programs  are  fair  and  useful  to  students  of all  racial
and  ethnic  backgrounds.”

1976-77  SAT  Registration  form.

It should  be  noted  that  nothing  is said  about  that  fact  that
such  information  will  almost  certainly  be  used  to  facilitate
reverse  discrimination.  However,  in a  sense,  the  statement  may

not  be  deceptive  in its effects,  since  the  overwhelming  majority
of  students  are  probably  quite  well  aware  that  this  is  the
purpose  for  which  the  questions  are  asked.

One  cannot  help  but  wonder  what  the  nexi  step  is.  There
are  various  possibilities.  A black  person  could  be deemed  to  be
anyone  with  “one-eighth  or  more  of African  or  Negro  blood”
or  even  any  “ascertainable”  amount  of Negro  blood;  although
an  American  Indian  was not considered  a  colored  person  in the
State  of  Virginia  if  he  had  only  one-sixteenth  or  less  of
American  Indian  blood.  Loving  v.  Virginia,  388  U.S.  1, 5 n.4
(1967);  McLaughlin  v.  Florida,  379  U.S.  184,  187  n.6  (1964).
Perhaps,  for  the  immediate  future,  perpetrators  of reverse
discrimination  in  the  professional  schools  admission  arena  wiill
be content  with  the  kind  of questions  now  asked  on  the  Medical
College  Application  Service  form  and  that  used  by  the  Law
School  Data  Assembly  Service.  Those  questionnaires  merely
ask  the  race  of the  applicant  without  going  into  fractional  parts
of his ancestry.  Of course,  if the decision  below  is reversed,  thus
putting  the  imprimatur  of legality  on  preferential  admissions,
such  programs  will  inevitably  expand  and  there  will  be much
desire  on  the  part  of  many  applicants  to  get  recognized  as
minority  group  persons  and  receive  the  benefits  flowing  from
The  decision  makers,  at  that  point,  may  find  it
such  status.
necessary  to adopt  more stringent  tests to determine  just who is
a  member  of what  racial  group.  For  guidance  in  finding  the
appropriate  criteria  and  applying  them,  they  might  well  wish to
cull  literature  on  the  considerable  body  of experience  obtained
in  such  matters  in Germany  during  the  1930s  and  early  1940s:
“There  are  already  cases  of  individuals  who  redefine
themselves  in  this  country  for  some  benefit—the  part
American  Indian  who  becomes  an  Indian  for  some  public
purpose,  the  person  with  a Spanish-surnamed  mother  who
now  finds  it  advantageous  to  change  his  or  her  name;
conceivably  the  black  who  has  passed  as  white  and  who
may  reclaim  black  status  for  an  educational  or  employ-
ment. benefit.  We  have  not  yet  reached  the  degraded

condition  of the  Nuremberg  laws,  but  undoubtedly  we  will
have  to  create  a  new  law  of  personal  ethnic  and  racial
Status  to  define  just  who  is eligible  for  these  benefits,  to
replace  the  laws  we  have  banned  to  determine  who  should
be  subject  to  discrimination.”

N.  Glazer,  Affirmative  Discrimination  200  (1975).

For  additional  discussion  and  more  examples  on  this topic,
we  respectfully  refer  the  Court  to  the  Brief Amicus  of Timothy
J.  Hoy,  Point  II.

N.  Reverse  Discrimination  Is a  Racist  Concepi

The  defenders  of reverse  discrimination  often  accuse  their
opponents  of being  “racists.”  However,  a  look  at  any  diction-
ary’s  definition  of  racism  is  most  illuminating.
Contrary  to
popular  belief,  that  word  does  not  mean  “racial  hatred.”
Rather,  it means  a belief that  one  race  or  ethnic  group—usually
that  of  the  racist—is  inherently  superior  to  another.2°  There-
fore,  it would  be quite possible  for a person  to  believe  that  some
particular  racial  group  is inferior  to  his own,  while  regarding  its
members  with  charity  and  benevolence.

One  reason  often  advanced  for  favoring  reverse  dis-
crimination  is  that  without  it,  supposedly,  the  number  of
minority  group  persons  selected  in  any  competitive  process
would  be  minuscule  or  non-existent.
If  there  are  in  fact
minority  candidates  who  possess  the  minimum  qualifications  to
be  selected,  however,  to  assume  automatically  that  choosing
only  the  best-qualified  candidates  will  reject  most  of  the
minority  applicants  is  to  assume  that  these  minority  group
persons  are  indeed  actually  inferior,  and  thus  must  get  some

20 In  addition  to the standard  dicuonanies,  this definiuon  also
ars in
such works as T.F. Hoult (ed.).  Dictionary
of Modern  Sociology  262 (1969)
and  GA.  &  AG.  Theodorson  (eds.),  Modern  Dictionary  of Sociology  329
(1969).  True to form,  however,  Petitioners’
“racism,”  at  58,  despite  the  fact  that  it  is  Petitioners  who  maintain  that
minontues  cannot  gain  admission  to medical  school  in any but token  numbers
a merit system.
under

Brief accuses  their opponents  of

82

83

sort  of  preference  if  their  race  or  ethnic  group  is  to  be
represented  in the  final  body  of persons  chosen.  However,  that
is  the  essence  of  racism:  to  believe  that  the  members  of  a
particular  group  are  or  tend  to  be inferior,  even  if one  does  not
hate  them  as  a  result,  but  want  to  do  everything  possible  to
ameliorate  their  lot.

Under  the  criterion  that  the  best-qualified  candidate  is to
be  picked,  it is to  be expected  that  since  minority  group  people
really  are  individuals  just as  anyone  else,  some  will  be very  well
qualified  and  others  will  not  be.  And  therefore,  it  will  be
expected  that  under  non-discriminatory  selection,  a  goodly
number  of minority  group  persons  will  be chosen  on  their  own
Thus,  to  be  a  non-racist,  one  must  Oppose  reverse
merits.
It is true,  of course,  that  the  present  effects  of
discrimination.
past  discrimination,  deprivation  and  poverty  have  reduced  the
number  of  well-qualified  minority  people  in  most  fields  of
endeavor,  and  may  also  have  kept  even  some  of  the  most
talented  from  developing  their  skills  to  the  utmost.  But  this  is
the  kind  of  problem  that  legitimate  and  proper  affirmative
action  was  designed  to  ameliorate.
It  is  not  an  excuse  for
discriminating  in  favor  of  a  minority  person,  and  against  an
innocent  member  of another  group.

It  is  common  knowledge  that  under  the  best-qualified
candidate  criterion,  minority  people  have  risen  to  the  top  in
fields  such  as  entertainment  and  professional  sports,  and  are
recognized  as  superior  to  their  non-minority  competitors.  If the
defenders  of  reverse  discrimination  are  really  saying  that  the
excellence  of blacks  or  Hispanics  is limited  to  these  particular
areas  of  endeavor,  so  that  preferences  are  the  only  way  for
them  to  gain  prestige  in  other  pursuits,  a  more  insulting  and
patronizing  form  of racism  would  be  hard  to  imagine.

To  quote  the  president  of Boston  University:

“[O]jnly  a  sexist  or  racist  could  believe  that  women  or
members  of minorities  are  in fact  inferior  to every  one  else,
and  would  be  unable  to  compete  on  an  equal  basis  if

judged  by performance  alone.  ...  Rejecting  excellence  in
the  interest  of  women  and  minorities  is  in  effect  a  con-
descending  adoption  of a  lower  standard  for  them.”

J.  R.  Silber,  “The  Need  for  Elite  Education,”  Har-

per’s,  June  1977,  22,  24.

O.  Reverse  Discrimination  Is Morally  Wrong

One  of  the  philosophical  bases  of  the  position  of  these
Amici  is  found  in  the  writings  of Aristotle,  who  distinguished
two  different  varieties  of justice.  Commutative  (also  called
corrective  or  rectificatory)  justice  prescribes  that  one  who  has
harmed  another  by  means  of a  civil  wrong,  such  as  a  tort  or
breach  of  contract,  “should  compensate  the  victim  in  an
amount  equal  to  the  harm  thus  caused.”  The  other  type,
distributive  justice,  “is  designed  to  distribute  the  honors  or
rewards  of a  society  among  its  members  in  proportion  to  their
merits.”  E.W.  Patterson,  Jurisprudence  391  (1953);  E.  Barker,
The  Politics  of Aristotle  363  (Oxford  Galaxy,  paperback  1962).
Clearly,  those  who  espouse  preferential  treatment  are  confusing
these  two  kinds  of justice.  They  are  applying  the  principle  of
rectificatory  justice  to  a  situation  where  distributive  justice,
which  in  the  Aristotelian  scheme  corresponds  to  the  merit
system,  should  govern.
Despite  the  fact  that  our  society  has
indeed  done  grievous  harm  to  members  of minority  groups  and
women,  by denying  them  their  rightful  opportunities  for  self-
fulfillment,  the  remedy  does  not  lie  in doing  the  same  thing-  -
ironically  in the  name  of justice —to innocent  members  of other
groups.

Another  underpinning  for our  beliefs  is found  in a concept
developed  by  the  late  American  legal  philosopher  Edmond
Cahn,  a  professor  at  New  York  University  School  of Law.

Cahn  believed  that  we  can  ascertain  what  is just  and  right
only  from  the  “consumer  perspective.”  He  contrasted  this
viewpoint  with  the  “imperial”  or  “official”  perspective.  Cahn
was  not  referring  to  what  we  now  call  “consumer  protection”  or
“consumerism.”  He  meant  the  viewpoint  of  the  individuals

84

85

affected  by  the  problem  involved  and  by  efforts  to  solve  it.
Cahn  saw  that  it  is  always  one  or  more  individuals  who
consume  the  results  of the  decisions  made  by officialdom,  and
that  the justice  or  injustice  of governmental  decisions  (or other
authoritative  actions)  must  be  assessed  from  the  standpoint  of
those  individuals,  not  from  that  of the  decision  siakers.

This  way  of looking  at  things,  said  Cahn,  can  be compared
‘to  the “official  or  imperial  perspective”  —that  of administrators,
bureaucrats,  legislators  or  other  decision  makers  in the  public or
private  sectors.  These  people,  often  with  the  best  of motives,
tend  to  ignore,  downgrade  or  violate  individuals’  interests  in
order—so  they say—to  best  serve  those  of “society,”  or  of some
governmental  or  other  entity  such  as  a  school,  association,
corporation,  municipality  or  group  of persons.  That  this  may
often  eniail  cruelty  and  inflict  suffering  on  specific  individuals,
with  or  without  visible  benefits  to  the entity  involved,  is deemed
irrelevant  by those  who  take  this  perspective.  At  the  most,  they
say,  such  unfortunate  factors  must  be  subordinated  to  various
paramount  considerations,  which  include  “the  necessity  for
showing  respect  for  expert  judgment  and  administrative  con-
venience”  and  “the  necessity  of leaving  certain  wrongs,  how-
ever  grievous  they  may  be,  to  the  province  of morals.”  These
and  other  examples  of  phrases  and  maxims  used  to  justify
injustices  to  individuals  (although  they  have  their  proper  uses
as  well)  were  set  forth  by Professor  Cahn.  See  E.  Cahn,  The
Predicament  of Democratic  Man  17-42  (1961);  E. Cahn,  “Law
in  the  Consumer  Perspective,”  112  U.  Pa.  L.  Rev.  1 (1963).

The  application  of this  reasoning  to  reverse  discrimination
is  obvious.
Whether  or  not  it  is  “true”  discrimination  is  a
question  that  must  be  determined  from  the  standpoint  of the
victim,  not  from  that  of society  in  general.  Viewed  from  this
proper  perspective  —that  of the  true  consumer  of the  act—it  is
discrimination  as  injurious  and  cruel,  if not  more  so,  as  the
original  acts  of bigotry  which  we  vainly  hoped  would  be halted
by  the  passage  of civil  rights  laws.  The  victim  of any  act  of
discrimination,  be  he  a  member  of  the  majority  or  of  a

minority,  is truly  a  consumer  of injustice.  If  he asks a
government  agency  to  counteract  some  decision  maker's  policy
of reverse discrimination,
and 1s rebuffed  —perhaps  because  the
policy  was implemented  at  the behest  of that same  agency — his
lot is even  more  bitter.  This  particular
fate  does  not befall

icclens

89

using  a  shorthand  term,  for obviously  a  “suspect  classification”
is  one that is  suspected  of possessing  some illegal  quality.  This
quality  is  of course discrimination.  In the  instant  case,  Petition-
ers  have  admittedly  discriminated  on  the  grounds  of race,  but
seek  to defend  such discrimination  as serving  some  higher  social
good.  Thus, in the instant  case, the suspicion  has been  borne  out
at  the very start  of the analysis  that this  Court  must  perform.
The  “classification”  (if such a  neutral  term  must  be  applied  to
it)  is  not  merely  suspected  of  being  discriminatory—it  is
discriminatory.
While  classification  may  be  neutral,  outright
race  discrimination,  is,  by definition,  not  neutral,  and  is there-
fore  prohibited  by the  Equal  Protection  Clause.

To point out  this  indubitable  fact  is not  to  beg the question,
for despite it, Petitioners  could,  in theory,  still  prevail  on the
equal  protection  issue  if they  could  nonetheless  show  that  such
discrimination  is justified  by an  appropriate  quantum  of gov-
ernmental  need.  These  Amici,  as  will  be shown,  believe  that,  if
not  unconstitutional  per  se,  this  instance  of race  discrimination
is unconstitutional  unless  it  could  pass  muster  under  the  “strict
scrutiny”  test.  Therefore.  these  Amici  respectfully  urge  that  the

POINT  TWO:  REVERSE  DISCRIMINATION  IS  NO
MORE  PERMISSIBLE  THAN  TRADITIONAL  DIS-
CRIMINATION;  IT  IS  PATENTLY  OFFENSIVE  TO  THE
CONSTITUTION,  RELEVANT  STATUTES  AND  WEIGHT
OF  JUDICIAL  AUTHORITY

A.  Petitioners’  Line  of  Argument  on  Equal  Protection  Ques-

tion  is Fundamentally  Misleading  and  Incorrect

Because  conventional  analysis  of  equal  protection  prob-
lems  talks  of  “classifications”  by  race,  Petitioners  under-
standably  seek  to  avoid  the  consequences  of their  violation  of
Respondent's  rights  by  characterizing  their  actions  as  a
“classification”  instead  of  as  discrimination.
These  Amici
believe  that  even  if  Petitioners’  actions  are regarded  as  a
“classification”  on  the  basis  of  race,  such  actions  violate  the
Equal!  Protection  Clause  whether  they  are  viewed  under  the
“strict  scrutiny”  test  accorded  to  “suspect  classifications,”  the
“rational  basis”  test,  or  some  hybrid  intermediate  test.  How-
ever,  these  Amici  cannot  help  but  feel  that  the  neutral,  non-
pejorative  label  of “classification”  is an  ill-fitted  description  for
Petitioners’  conduct  here  —a  circumlocution  that  provides  them
with  an  undeserved  semantic  shelter.

When  a  court  analyzes  whether  a  particular  instance  of
differential  treatment  of  persons  on  the  ground  of  race  con-
stitutes  a  “suspect  classification”  of  such  persons,  it  is  really

ie Oe eee

90

91

However,  whether  this  Court  applies  the  “compelling  state
interest”  test,  the  “rational  basis”  test,  or  a  hybrid  test,  none  of
these  could  be satisfied  by the  medical  school.  There  can  be no
“compelling  state  interest”  to  commit  racial  discrimination  in
student  admissions  against  any  group  of applicants  to  a state
university.
Nor  can  such  discrimination  have  a  “rational
relationship”  to  the  performance  of  any  legitimate  govern-
mental  function,  let  alone  satisfy  any  “intermediate”  test.

The  following  hypothetical  may  clarify  matters:  Let  us
suppose  that  members  of  religious,  racial,  or  national  origin
minority  group  “x”  can  be  shown  to  have  a  very  high  rate  of
violent  criminality  involving  weapons.  Would  it be  permissible
to  make  them  carry  official  photo-identification  cards  which
would  have  to  be  flashed  to  the  police  on  demand,  or  to  allow
the  police  to  frisk  members  of this  group,  and  enter  their  homes
for  “no-knock”  inspections,  whenever  a  patrolman  thought  it
appropriate?  Obviously  not,  for  despite  the  involvement  of
what  might  well  be  deemed  a  compelling  state  interest  (the
need  to  counteract  crime)  and  despite  the  near-certainty  that
such  dragnet  searches  would  yield  significant  numbers  of
switchblade  knives,  sawed-off  shotguns  and  handguns,  Con-
stitutional  rights  cannot  be  suspended  on  the  basis  of religious
affiliation,  race,  or  national  origin.

To  make  the  argument  stronger,  suppose  that  law  enforce-
ment  officials  pointed  out,  in  defense  of such  measures,  irrefu-
table  statistics  showing  that  the  overwhelming  majority  of
crimes  perpetrated  by  members  of group  “x”  are  perpetrated
against  their  fellow  “*x-ists.”

Thus,  the  recommended  repressive  measures  would  ac-
tually  be  primarily  protecting  “x-ists”  rather  than  the  general
Surely,  then,  it  would  be  urged,  these  devices  would
public.
comport  with  the  Constitution,  since  the  supposedly  victumized
group  was  being  helped  rather  than  harmed  by  these

departures
unfortunately
Amendment  standards,  but  these  Amici  believe  that  the  courts

There  is an  additional  insight  that  can  be  gleaned  from  the
Korematsu  case —an  insight  into  the  ominous  implications  of an
affirmance  by the  Court  of the  decision  below.  In  Korematsu,
the  petitioner,  an  American  citizen,  had  been  convicted  of
violating  a  1942  military  order  which  directed  that  all  persons
of Japanese  ancestry  were  to  be excluded  from  a  certain  area  in
the  Western  United  States,  and  the  Court  affirmed  the  con-
In  his  dissent,  Mr.  Justice  Jackson  explained  why  he
viction.
believed  that  the  Court's  decision  was  more  pernicious  than  the
discrimination  by  the  Executive  Branch  which  had  led  to  the
litigation.

Since  that  case  involved  the  Federal  Government,  it  was
the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth  Amendment  rather  than
the  Equal  Protection  Clause  of the  Fourteenth  that  was  at  issue,
but  the  Constitutional  standard  was—and  still  is—the  same.
Weinberger  v.  Weisenfeld,  420  U.S.  636,  638  n.2  (1975).  We
therefore  commend  to  the  Court  Justice  Jackson's  words:

“Much  is  said  of  the  danger  to  liberty  from  the  Army
program  for  deporting  and  detaining  these  citizens  of
Japanese  extraction.  But  a judicial  construction  of the  due
process  clause  that  will  sustain  this  order  is  a  far  more
subtle  blow  to  liberty  than  the  promulgation  of the  order
itself.  A  military  order,  however  unconstitutional,  is  not
Even
apt  to  last  longer  than  the  military  emergency.
during  that  period  a  succeeding  commander  may  revoke  it
all.  But  once  a judicial  opinion  rationalizes  such  an  order
to  show  that  it  conforms  to  the  Constitution,  or  rather
rationalizes  the  Constitution  to  show  that  the  Constitution
sanctions  such  an  order,  the  Court  for  all  time  has  vali-
dated  the  principle  of  racial  discrimination  in  criminal
The
procedure  and  of  transplanting  American  citizens.

needed  or  not,  is  not  to  be  given  up  easily:  Black  students  occupied
school  buildings  and  demanded  that  the  privileges  given  on  the  basis  of
race  be  retained.
This  is  part  of  the  evil  of  the  creation  of  especially
benefited  ethnic  and  racial  categories.”  N.  Glazer,  Affirmative  Dis-
crimination  199  (1975)

principle  then  lies  about  like  a  loaded  weapon  ready  for
the  hand  of  any  authority  that  can  bring  forward  a
plausible  claim  of an  urgent  need.  Every  repetition  imbeds
that  principle  more  deeply  in  our  law  and  thinking  and
expands  it to  new  purposes.  All  who  observe  the  work  of
ceurts  are  familiar  with  what  Judge  Cardozo  described  as
‘the  tendency  of a  principle  to  expand  itself  to  the  limit  of
its  logic.”  A  military  commander  may  overstep  the  bounds
of constitutionality,  and  it is an  incident.  But  if we  review
and  approve,  that  passing  incident  becomes  the  doctrine  of
There  it  has  a  generative  power  of  its
the  Constitution.
own,  and  all  that  it  creates  will  be  in  its  own  image.
Nothing  better  illustrates  this  danger  than  does  the  Court's
opinion  in  this  case.”

323  U.S.  at  245-6.  (footnote  of dissent  omitted. )

We  respectfully  direct  the  Court's  attention  to  a  serious
problem  concerning  the  decision  below:  it is favorable  toward
reverse  discrimination  in  that  it  leaves  open  the  door  for
Petitioners  or  any  other  perpetrators  to  justify  such  actions  on
the  ground  of “necessity”  in  the  future.
It should  have  barred
the  use  of preferential  admissions  absolutely.  Even  the  propo-
nents  of these  programs  object  to  the  “uncertainty,  turmoil  and
confusion”  which  will  inevitably  stem  from  this  aspect  of  the
Law  School  Deans’  Brief  on  Certiorari,  31.
ruling  below.
Although  this  time  the  perpetrators  have  not  met  their  “bur-
den”  imposed  by  the  court  below  of  justifying  their  reverse
discrimination,  18  Cal.  3d  at  53,  553  P.2d  at  1165,  132  Cal.
Rptr.  at  693,  who  knows  what  the  future  will  bring?  Certainly
others  will  try  desperately,  through  bigger  and  better  “  Brandeis
briefs,”  and  endless  parades  of witnesses,  to  meet  this  burden.
Only  this  Court  can  prevent  such  situations  from  arising
repeatedly.

Furthermore,  such  litigative  activities  may  not  be limited  to
Others  may  come
perpetrators  of  reverse  discrimination.
forward  to  prove  “compelling  state  interests”  that  excuse
“traditional”  discrimination  against  some  minority  group.

decision  below,  with  appropriate  modifications,  so  as  to  per-
manently  “close  the  door”  on  the  possibility  of justifying  the
kind  of scheme  under  review  here.

quoted  approvingly  the  District  Court’s  finding  of  fact  that

from  positions,”  or  “to  appoint  Negroes  in numbers  proportion-
al  to  the  school  population.”  Rather,  the  Board  sought  to

Another  case  cited  by  Petitioners  shows  great  judicial
sensitivity  toward  the  gross  inequities  involved  even  in  court-
ordered  remedial  preferences.  In  Carter  v.  Gallagher,  452  F.2d
315  (8th  Cir.,  en  banc  1971),  cert.  denied,  406  U.S.  950  (1972),
the  court,  while  imposing  a  remedial  preference,  nonetheless
decried  preferential  treatment  (452  F.2d  at  330),  struck  a
provision  of  the  District  Court's  decree  ordering  an  absolute
minority  remedial  preference  (at  331),  termed  its  own  less
drastic  relief  a  non-quota  system  because  of  its  temporary
nature  (at  330),  and  stated  that  such  relief  would  not  “neces-
sarily  result  in  hiring  less  qualified  minority  persons”  (at  331  ).
(The  Court  of  Appeals  ordered  that  one  out  of  each  three
firefighters  hired  be  minority  group  members  until  twenty  of the
latter  had  been  added  to  the  Minneapolis  Fire  Department. ) 24

(1935).

stitutional  authority  for  its  evident  belief  that  minority  groups

U.S.  1  (1971),  a  school  segregation  case  cited  by  Petitioners

If  the  Court
rejects  quotas  in a  public  school  situation  where  no  pupil  and  no

Petitioners

also  cite  other  decisions  approving  race-
conscious  remedies  for  desegregating  and  improving  racial
balance  in public  schools,  such  as  Keyes  v.  School  District  No.  /,
413  U.S.  189  (1973)  and  Offermann  v.  Nitkowski,  378  F.2d  22
(2d  Cir.  1967).  These  cases  are  not  controlling,  for  there  the
issue  was  what  school  a child  would  be assigned  to,  not  whether
he would  be allowed  to  attend  school  at  all.  In the  instant  case,
Petitioners’  conduct  constituted  discrimination  because  it  was
exclusionary  toward  Respondent,  rather  than  a  mere  rearrange-
ment;  and  Petitioners’  efforts  to  equate  the  two  are  most
unconvincing.
(Neither  are  their  efforts  to  show,  at  74-5  of
their  Brief,  that  the  Court's  use  of the  rational  basis  test  in these
and  similar  cases  is authority  for  the  use  of that  test  [instead  of
the  strict  scrutiny  standard]  in  the  instant  case. ) 25

cases  are  authority  for  justifying  a  kind  of  employment  dis-
crimination  which  Petitioners  seek  to  analogize  to  their  own
conduct  toward  Respondent.  The  analogy  is  false.  Not  only
are  black  teachers  as  well  as  whites  liable  to  be  affected  by any
such  court-ordered  remedial  actions  (which  of  course  are
simply  one  variant  of court-ordered  preferences  to  correct  past
proven  discrimination),
but—as
explicitly
held—faculty  hiring  during  desegregation  must  in  all  cases

With  respect  to  Petitioners’  heavy  reliance  on  United
Jewish  Organizations  v.  Carey,
—__,  51  L.Ed.  2d 229
(1977),  we  wish  to  emphasize  that  the  mere  fact  that  a
Supreme  Court  decision  deals  in  some  way  with  reverse  dis-
crimination  does  not  ipso facto  make  it dispositive  of the  issues
involved  in  the  instant  case.
United  Jewish  Organizations  is
simply  not  in  point.

_U.S.

It  arose  under  the  Voting  Rights  Act,  a  specific  statute
which  deals  with  a  very  different  matter  form  professional
school  admissions.

Under  the  decision,  no  one  loses  his  vote  entirely.  Each
person’s  individual  right  to  cast  a  ballot  is preserved.  In  short,
in  United  Jewish  Organizations,  individuals’  rights  are  pre-
served,  but  in  the  instant  case,  the  individually  possessed  right
to  be  free  from  racial  discrimination  in  the  admissions  process
of a  state  medical  school  was  /ost  by Bakke.

In  the  voting  case,  the  plaintiffs  sued  because  they  wanted
their  “group  rights”  to  be  the  same  as  those  of other  groups.  In
the  instant  case,  however,  Bakke  sued  merely  to  vindicate  his
individual  rights,  which  had  been  grievously  violated.
The
instant  litigation  concerns  individual  rather  than  group  rights.
Petitioners  are  wrongly  alleging  that  some  people  have  “group
rights”  which  cancel  out  others’  individual  rights.

Furthermore,  the  dilution  of the  “group  voting  power”  of
plaintiffs  in  United  Jewish  Organizations  cannot  be  compared
with  the  exclusion  of an  individual  from  the  medical  school  in
the  instant  case.

Since  there  is  no  “opinion  of the  Court”  in  United  Jewish
Organizations,  there  is no  authoritative  exposition  of the  reason-
ing  relied  on  by the  majority  of the  Justices.  For  that  reason,  it

would  be  best  to  limit  the  holding  of  United  Jewish  Organiza-
tions  to  the  specific  facts  therein.
However,  the  concurring
opinion  of  Mr.  Justice  Brennan,  51  L.Ed.  2d  at  248-54,  does
provide  an  eloquent  statement  of some  strong  arguments  U S.

its  supposed  beneficiaries.
conclusion  to  the  dissenting  opinion  of Mr.  Chief  Justice  Burger
contains  some  illuminating  comments  on  the  general  problem.

Morton  v.  Mancari,  417  U.S.  535  (1974)  cited  by Petition-
ers,  deals  with  a  political,  not  a  racial  preference;  those
American  Indians  who  are  not  members  of  Federally  recog-
nized  Indian  tribes  would  not  qualify  for  the  preferred  status.
417  US.  at  553  & n.24.  Morton  may  be said  to  hinge  on  the  sui
generis  status  of  Native  Americans,  and  the  Government's
unique  trusteeship  obligation  toward  them.

The  Brief  Amicus  of the  National  Association  of Affirma-
tive  Action  Officers  makes  but  a  single  point:  that  under
Arlington  Heights  v.  Metro  Housing  Corp.,
L.Ed.  2d  450  (1977)  and  Washington  v.  Davis,  426  US.  229
(1976),  the  preferences  in  the  instant  case  must  be  upheld
absent  proof of racially  discriminatory  intent  or  purpose  which,
the  National  Association  urges,  is not  found  here.

US.

,

But  by that  standard,  the  preferential  scheme  at  issue  must
be invalidated.  There  is no  need  to  prove  discriminatory  intent,
since  it  has  been  admitted  by  Petitioners.
The  confusion
evidently  afflicting  the  National  Association  is  that  the  dis-
criminatory  intent,  according  to  Petitioners,  is  being  exercised
for  a  noble  end.
As  does  Petitioners’  Brief,  the  Nationa!
Association’s  Brief  is  caught  up  in  a  semantic  muddle  with
respect  to  the true  meaning  of the  word  “invidious.”  Petitioners,
and  the  National  Association,  are  convinced  that  since  their
actions  are  not  motivated  by  hatred  for  whites,  they  are  ipso
facto  absolved  of culpability  for discrimination  under  the  Equal
Protection  Clause.  That,  we  submit,  cannot  be  so.
